title: An analytical self-consistent theory for the elastic moduli of granular materials
journal: Journal of the Mechanics and Physics of Solids
abstract: An accurate prediction of the effective elastic moduli from contact-scale properties is essential for multiscale constitutive modelling of granular materials. In the derivation of the moduli from microscopic characteristics, it is crucial that the localisation operator, which expresses relative displacements at contacts in terms of macroscopic deformation, satisfies the kinematic consistency condition. This study develops a self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory to predict the elastic moduli of two-dimensional isotropic granular assemblies of disk-shaped particles. The theory incorporates an explicitly enforced kinematic consistency condition, leading to a localisation operator that produces relative displacements consistent with the prescribed macroscopic strain, and consequently improved elastic moduli. The proposed SC-PF theory is evaluated using two-dimensional Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations performed under isotropic compression and pure shear loading. Owing to its enforcement of self-consistency, the SC-PF theory provides substantially improved predictions of both the displacement fluctuations and elastic moduli, across a range of coordination numbers and contact stiffness ratios. In particular, the SC-PF theory improves the prediction of the bulk and shear moduli, with relative deviations reduced by 69% and 65%, respectively, compared with the mean-field theory, and by 22% and 45% compared with the pair-fluctuation approach of Jenkins et al. (2005). Beyond improved accuracy, the SC-PF theory provides insight into how the geometric and mechanical characteristics of interparticle contacts govern macroscopic elastic behaviour, thereby advancing multiscale constitutive modelling of granular materials.

An accurate prediction of the effective elastic moduli from contact-scale properties is essential for multiscale constitutive modelling of granular materials. In the derivation of the moduli from microscopic characteristics, it is crucial that the localisation operator, which expresses relative displacements at contacts in terms of macroscopic deformation, satisfies the kinematic consistency condition. This study develops a self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory to predict the elastic moduli of two-dimensional isotropic granular assemblies of disk-shaped particles. The theory incorporates an explicitly enforced kinematic consistency condition, leading to a localisation operator that produces relative displacements consistent with the prescribed macroscopic strain, and consequently improved elastic moduli. The proposed SC-PF theory is evaluated using two-dimensional Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations performed under isotropic compression and pure shear loading. Owing to its enforcement of self-consistency, the SC-PF theory provides substantially improved predictions of both the displacement fluctuations and elastic moduli, across a range of coordination numbers and contact stiffness ratios. In particular, the SC-PF theory improves the prediction of the bulk and shear moduli, with relative deviations reduced by 69% and 65%, respectively, compared with the mean-field theory, and by 22% and 45% compared with the pair-fluctuation approach of Jenkins et al. (2005). Beyond improved accuracy, the SC-PF theory provides insight into how the geometric and mechanical characteristics of interparticle contacts govern macroscopic elastic behaviour, thereby advancing multiscale constitutive modelling of granular materials. Keywords Granular materials Micromechanics Elastic moduli Self-consistent theory Data availability Data will be made available on request. 1 Introduction Granular materials are prevalent in both natural and engineering contexts, serving crucial roles in agriculture, energy production, and geotechnical engineering. To understand and predict their mechanical behaviour, constitutive relationships have been developed through heuristic macro-scale approaches and micromechanical methods. In the macro-scale approach, granular materials are treated as continuous media through various simplifications and approximations, allowing the use of well-established continuum mechanics methods. However, when applied to systems with inherently discrete structures, such models cannot explicitly reflect the contact-level interactions, offering limited insight into the particle-scale mechanisms governing macroscopic behaviour. In contrast, micromechanical methods represent granular materials as assemblies of rigid particles interacting through contacts, thereby explicitly incorporating microstructural features and providing a more direct and intuitive understanding of the underlying mechanics. Micromechanical methods primarily investigate the relationships between macroscopic properties at the continuum level and the microscopic particle-scale interactions. These relationships can be derived using homogenisation techniques (Cambou et al., 2000; 1995; Kruyt and Rothenburg, 2004). For quasi-static deformations, the (increments of) stress and strain tensors at the macroscopic level are essential for describing the mechanical states of granular materials. At the microscopic level, the forces and relative displacements between particles in contact are of primary importance. Through averaging processes, the macroscopic stress tensor can be expressed in terms of micromechanical quantities, specifically contact forces and particle arrangements (see, for instance, Drescher and de Josselin de Jong (1972); Kruyt and Rothenburg (1996); Love (1927); Rothenburg and Selvadurai (1981); Weber (1966)). Similarly, the macroscopic strain tensor can be represented in terms of relative displacements at interparticle contacts (Bagi, 1996; Kruyt and Rothenburg, 1996; Zhao et al., 2025). Conversely, with additional assumptions, micro-scale relative displacements (or contact forces) can be obtained from the macro-scale strain (or stress) via (kinematic) localisation operations (Cambou et al., 2000; 1995; Nicot and Darve, 2005). The current study focuses on (linear) elastic properties at the continuum level of isotropic systems consisting of particles that interact elastically at contacts, analysed from a micromechanical perspective. At the macro-scale, the elastic response of the material is described by the effective elastic moduli, whereas at the micro-scale, the elastic behaviour of interparticle contacts is represented by two linear springs acting in the normal and tangential directions. The effective elastic moduli are of fundamental importance, as they govern the initial stiffness of granular materials, influence wave propagation, and provide the constitutive basis for many continuum models used in geotechnical and material design applications. A reliable micromechanical prediction of these moduli therefore plays a key role in linking particle-scale mechanics to macroscopic engineering behaviour. To obtain macroscopic elastic moduli from particle-scale properties, various approaches exist in the literature: (i) experiments (Hoque and Tatsuoka, 2004; Magnanimo et al., 2008; Wang and Mok, 2008; Wei et al., 2025), (ii) Discrete Element Method (DEM for short, as proposed by Cundall and Strack (1979)) simulations (Chen et al., 2025; Gong et al., 2019; Gu and Yang, 2013; Wu et al., 2022), and (iii) analytical methods (Bathurst and Rothenburg, 1988b; Jenkins et al., 2005; Kruyt and Rothenburg, 2002; 2014). The last approach is followed in this study. Among analytical approaches, the mean-field theory plays a central role in localisation operations, providing a framework for determining the relative displacement or contact force between two contacting particles (Bathurst and Rothenburg, 1988a; 1988b; Chang et al., 1995; 1990). This approach assumes that the relative displacement between the two particles is determined by the uniform strain field and the relative position vector of the particle centres. Once the relative displacement is determined, the corresponding contact force is derived from the micro-scale constitutive relation, i.e. the contact stiffness. The macroscopic stress is subsequently determined by averaging the contact forces. This process ultimately establishes the elastic relationship between macro-scale stress and strain tensors. A mean-field theory based on a uniform stress field can be formulated in a similar manner. In the present study, the term mean-field theory refers exclusively to the formulation based on a uniform strain field. Further details on the averaging and localisation operators within the homogenisation framework proposed by Cambou et al. (2000, 1995) are provided in Section 2.6 and 2.7. Further studies (Kruyt and Rothenburg, 1998; 2002) showed, analytically, and numerically by DEM simulations, that the elastic moduli based on the uniform strain assumption are over-estimated. The numerical results indicated that the predictions of the elastic moduli are accurate for dense granular systems with high coordination number, but are significantly over-predicted for loose granular systems (Kruyt et al., 2010; Kruyt and Rothenburg, 2002). Kruyt and Rothenburg (1998, 2002) derived rigorous bounds for the elastic moduli based on variational principles. The upper bound for the elastic moduli is obtained from the minimum potential energy principle based on the uniform strain assumption for a relative displacement field, while the lower bound is obtained with the minimum complementary energy principle based on the uniform stress assumption for a contact force field. A limitation of the uniform strain assumption is that the equilibrium conditions of particles are not generally satisfied. This indicates that the real displacements (rotations) of each particle generally deviate and fluctuate from the mean displacement field. As an attempt to achieve an approximate equilibrium, various approaches have been developed, such as the local-adjustment-field approach (Koenders, 1987; Kruyt, 2014; Kruyt et al., 2010) and the pair-fluctuation approach (Agnolin and Roux, 2007; Jenkins et al., 2005; La Ragione and Jenkins, 2007). These methods share the common feature of considering an inclusion consisting of one or more particles (a sub-assembly), where the displacements of particles within the inclusion are determined by force equilibrium (or by minimizing local potential energy) of a subsystem, while the displacements of surrounding particles follow the displacement prescribed by the uniform strain field. This process determines the fluctuations from the analysis of granular sub-assemblies. The key differences lie in the choice of the inclusion and the way in which the prescribed uniform strain is imposed. Note that the average strain corresponding to the displacement fluctuations should be zero to ensure the consistency with the original mean-field theory. Similarly, a uniform stress field-based method can be derived conjugately (Kruyt and Rothenburg, 2002). In the local-adjustment-field approach, Koenders (1987) evaluated two-dimensional granular systems by considering sub-assemblies consisting of a central particle and its neighbouring particles. The equilibrium of the central particle within each sub-assembly was used to derive corrections to the moduli predicted by the mean-field deformation assumption. Although internal displacement fluctuations were allowed to correct the uniform-strain prediction, interactions between sub-assemblies were neglected. Kruyt and Rothenburg (2002, 2004) considered the displacement and rotation fluctuations of an inclusion of a single particle. Results from DEM simulations of isotropic compression and pure shear tests showed that the lower and upper bounds for elastic moduli from the local-adjustment-field approach are narrower than those from the original mean-field theory, especially for loose granular assemblies and for dense assemblies subjected to shear loading. Kruyt et al. (2010) and Kruyt (2014) extended the inclusion to a sub-assembly, or shell, for two- and three-dimensional systems, respectively. The first-order sub-assembly consists of a reference particle, p, and its immediate neighbours, i.e., particles in direct contact with p. The i-th-order sub-assembly is then defined as the union of the ( i − 1 ) -th-order sub-assembly and the neighbours of the outer particles in that sub-assembly. They compared the bulk modulus K and the shear modulus G obtained from DEM simulations and from the “shell” approach, where the shell order i ranges from 0 to 4. The results show that larger i leads to a more accurate prediction of these moduli compared to the DEM results, while the moduli based on the uniform strain assumption differ strongly from the DEM results. The predictions also reveal the dependence of the bulk modulus K on the contact stiffness ratio k t/k n, where k t and k n are the shear and normal contact stiffnesses, respectively. The pair-fluctuation method proposed by Jenkins et al. (2005) and Agnolin and Roux (2007) considers an inclusion consisting of a pair of contacting particles. The displacements (and rotations) of this particle pair are decomposed into a mean-field component and a fluctuation relative to the mean field, while the surrounding particles are assumed to follow the mean-field strain. Studies by Agnolin and Kruyt (2008) and Agnolin and Roux (2008) assessed the elastic moduli derived from both the pair-fluctuation and local-adjustment-field methods against those obtained from DEM simulations across assemblies with varying coordination numbers and contact stiffness ratios. Their results indicate that incorporating relative displacement fluctuations generally improves the accuracy of predicted moduli. Following this framework, deformation localisation and failure behaviour can also be analysed (La Ragione et al., 2015; La Ragione et al., 2024). However, as will be addressed and clarified in this paper, the localisation operator used in the classical pair-fluctuation method suffers from a fundamental lack of consistency: the macroscopic strain recovered by averaging the localised relative displacements does not necessarily coincide with the macroscopic strain initially prescribed. Ensuring such consistency is essential for a physically coherent localisation process. The concept of a self-consistent field was first introduced in early quantum-mechanical theories (Hartree, 1928), where each particle was assumed to move in an effective field that represents the collective influence of all the other particles. The fundamental idea is that each constituent experiences an effective field generated by the ensemble to which it simultaneously contributes. This principle was later generalised to continuum mechanics and composite materials through the seminal works of Eshelby (1957), Hill (1965), and Budiansky (1965), and further summarised by Torquato et al. (2002). These studies established self-consistent schemes for estimating the effective elastic moduli of heterogeneous media by embedding each inclusion in an equivalent homogeneous medium whose properties are determined by requiring that the ensemble-averaged perturbation vanishes. Self-consistent ideas have been adapted to granular materials, where macroscopic behaviour emerges from discrete contact interactions. In such systems, fluctuations in contact forces, particle displacements, and rotations play a central role in governing the stiffness of granular materials. To address the inherently heterogeneous nature of granular materials, Misra and Chang (1993) proposed an early micromechanical self-consistent averaging scheme in which local cells with different stiffnesses are treated as inhomogeneities embedded in an effective medium. In their method, strain fluctuations inside inhomogeneities are represented as local strain perturbations through an Eshelby-type (Eshelby, 1957) transformation tensor. Each cell is associated with a particle and its nearest neighbouring particles, and a local stress–strain relation is defined at the cell level. The local strain is then related to the overall macroscopic strain through a concentration tensor, and the effective elastic moduli are estimated from volume averages of the local stress and strain fields. This approach improves upon the mean-field strain assumption by incorporating local heterogeneity, although discrepancies with numerical simulations remain. Moreover, the derivation of the local stiffness tensor requires contact geometry and connectivity information around each particle, and the final effective stiffness is obtained from an implicit equation that generally requires numerical solution rather than yielding a closed-form expression. More recently, self-consistent concepts have also been adapted to describe specific microstructural components in granular assemblies. For example, Gupta et al. (2024) modelled the strong force network as an inclusion phase embedded within a compliant weak network, using an Eshelby-type (Eshelby, 1957) analysis to capture their interactions. Their formulation predicts macroscopic elasticity through an inclusion-matrix homogenisation approach. However, the method relies heavily on DEM-calibrated inputs, such as the stiffness of strong and weak subnetworks, and is limited to frictionless granular assemblies. This study develops a self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory for predicting the effective bulk and shear elastic moduli of isotropic two-dimensional granular assemblies of disk-shaped particles. Contacts at the outer boundary of small sub-assemblies are assigned different (unspecified) contact stiffnesses, hereafter referred to as self-consistent contact stiffnesses, as they are determined by enforcing consistency with the overall imposed strain. An analytical localisation operator is subsequently derived that links macroscopic strain to interparticle relative displacements. The resulting SC-PF formulation provides a microstructurally informed analytical scheme suitable for granular assemblies across a broad range of coordination numbers and contact stiffness ratios. The statistical characteristics of the contact network required by the theory are extracted from DEM simulations, and the predictions of the SC-PF theory are systematically compared with DEM results to assess its accuracy. The outline of the paper is as follows. The micromechanical concepts, including the geometrical description of the contact network, particle-scale kinematics, and a brief introduction to the homogenisation scheme and the mean-field theory, are presented in Section 2. In Section 3, the formulation and derivation of the self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory are developed. In Section 4, the theoretical results and assumptions are analysed using the results from DEM simulations. These simulations are employed to illustrate the key micromechanical concepts and to provide validation data for evaluating the effective elastic moduli predicted by the theories. Section 5 discusses the contributions of this study and the open issues on this topic. 2 Micromechanics Two-dimensional isotropic granular assemblies of disk-shaped particles with poly-disperse particle radii are considered here, for simplicity. The approach described here can be employed as a basis for the more complex three-dimensional case or for anisotropic systems. The focus is on small-strain, linear elastic behaviour. The dependence of the elastic moduli on confining pressure is not explicitly considered. The effect of pressure can be accounted for by an appropriate choice of the pressure-dependent interparticle contact stiffnesses (Digby, 1981; Goddard, 1990; Walton, 1987). 2.1 Contact network The particles in granular materials generally interact only at interparticle contacts. The properties of the contact network are therefore important. One of the primary characteristics of the interparticle contact network is coordination number Z, i.e. the average number of contacts per particle. The coordination number is defined as Z = 2 N c / N p , where N c and N p are the number of contacts and the number of particles in the system under consideration, respectively. The solid fraction φ is defined as the ratio of the area occupied by the particles A p (in the two-dimensional case considered here) to the total system area A (including particles and voids), i.e. φ = A p / A . The contact density m A = N c / A is related to solid fraction φ, coordination number Z and the average of the particle radius squared, R 2 ‾ , by (1) m A = φ Z 2 π R 2 ‾ . The following geometrical characteristics are important for the subsequent derivations, see also Fig. 1 a. The radius of the disk-shaped particle p is Rp . The centre of particle p is denoted by X p . For a contact between two particles p and q, the branch vector l pq is the vector connecting their centres, i.e. l p q = X q − X p . The vector from the centre of particle p to the contact point corresponding to particles p and q is denoted by r pq and r qp is defined analogously. Note that l p q = r p q − r q p . The normal and tangential directions at such a contact are denoted by n pq and t pq , respectively, with n q p = − n p q and t q p = − t p q . For the rigid disk-shaped particles considered here, r p q = R p n p q and r q p = R q n q p (for zero particle “overlap”). The orientation of contact pq, θpq , is defined as the angle between the branch vector l pq and a reference direction. Contacts with similar orientations can be grouped into bins, Bi , each representing a range of orientations centred around θi (with the subscript i denoting the bin index). The contact distribution function E(θ) (Horne, 1965) is defined such that it gives the probability that the orientation θ(c) of a contact c lies in an interval of width Δθ centred around θ. For the two-dimensional isotropic systems considered here, E iso ( θ ) = 1 2 π . The conditional contact distribution function, also referred to as the structure function, e(α | θ) (Duan et al., 2025; Jenkins et al., 2005), gives the probability of finding a contact with orientation α, given that a neighbouring contact exists with orientation θ: (2) e ( α i ∣ θ j ) Δ α i ≡ M ( α i , θ j ) ∑ k = 1 n M ( α k , θ j ) = M i j ∑ k M k j . Here, n is the number of bins, M ( α i , θ j ) = M i j quantifies the number of contact pairs (in the whole granular assembly) in which one contact is oriented in the bin i (centred at αi ) and a neighbouring contact, on the same particle, is oriented in the bin j (centred at θj , see also Fig. 1b; note that contact pairs p − q − r 1 and r 1 − q − p are distinguished as different). A more detailed definition of e(α | θ) is provided in Duan et al. (2025). The function e(α | θ) accounts for the spherical exclusion of contact orientations arising from the presence of neighbouring contacts. It involves configurations of three particles, thereby capturing the local geometric structure of the granular assembly. Since e(α | θ) is a conditional probability density function, it follows from Eq. (2) that e(α | θ) satisfies the normalisation condition ∫ − π + π e ( α | θ ) d α = 1 . For a sufficiently large number of particles in a granular assembly, it can be assumed that e(α | θ) is central symmetric (as indicated by the results in Duan et al. (2025)), i.e., (3) e ( α + π | θ + π ) = e ( α | θ ) . For isotropic granular systems, e(α | θ) only depends on the angle between the contact pair β = α − θ , and therefore, (4) e ( α | θ ) = e iso ( β ) , with e iso(β) (for isotropic systems) defined as (5) e iso ( β i ) Δ β i ≡ ∑ j M j ⊕ i , j ∑ k ∑ l M k l , where j ⊕ i = ( ( j + i ) mod n ) denotes the circular addition (mod-n) of bin indices. The numerator thus counts all contact pairs whose orientation differences are β, while the denominator is the total number of counted pairs, ensuring the normalisation condition ∫ − π + π e iso ( β ) d β = 1 . From symmetry considerations M i j = M j i , it is expected that (6) e iso ( − β ) = e iso ( β ) . For future reference, the moments of the probability density function e iso(β) are defined by (7) μ m ≡ ( Z − 1 ) ∫ − π + π e iso ( β ) cos m β d β , where m is a non-negative integer. Since e iso(β) is a probability density function, it follows that μ 0 = Z − 1 . Moments of e iso(β) characterise the conditional contact distribution function and arise in the analytical theory in Section 3.2. Examples of the conditional contact distribution function e iso(β) and its moments, extracted from results of DEM simulations for granular assemblies with coordination numbers Z = 3.46 , 4.02, and 4.48, are given in Section 4.2. 2.2 Equilibrium conditions The equilibrium conditions consist of force and moment equilibrium equations. No contact couples are considered here. The force equilibrium equations for particle p are (8) ∑ q ∈ C p f p q = 0 , where the summation is over contacts c in the set Cp consisting of particles q that are in contact with particle p. The moment equation for particle p is ∑ q ∈ C p r p q × f p q = 0 . For the two-dimensional case of disk-shaped particles considered here, this condition can be expressed as (9) R p ∑ q ∈ C p t p q · f p q = 0 . 2.3 Kinematics and fluctuations The (small) displacement of the centre of particle p is denoted by U p , while its rotation is Ω p . The relative displacement (or contact deformation) Δ pq at a contact between particles p and q, consisting of contributions from the displacements of the particle centres and from particle rotations, is given by (10) Δ p q = ( U q − U p ) + ( Ω q × r q p − Ω p × r p q ) = ( U q − U p ) ︸ δ p q + ( − R p Ω p − R q Ω q ) ︸ ρ p q t p q . In this expression, δ pq denotes the contribution of the (vectorial) relative displacement between the particle centres, while ρpq represents, in the two-dimensional case, the scalar contribution associated with the relative particle rotation. The kinematics of the particles can be decomposed into contributions due to the imposed mean strain field, characterised by the displacement gradient tensor α and a mean rotation Ω ‾ , and fluctuations in the particle displacement, u p , and particle rotation, ωp : (11) U p = α · X p + u p Ω p = Ω ‾ + ω p . Here, the average rotation Ω ‾ is determined by the antisymmetric part of the displacement gradient tensor α , i.e. Ω ‾ = ( α 21 − α 12 ) / 2 , consistent with Zhao and Kruyt (2021). The total relative displacement δ pq and relative rotation ρpq between particles p and q can thus be decomposed into contributions due to the mean field, δ ‾ p q and ρ ‾ p q , and due to the fluctuations, δ ^ p q and ρ ^ p q , respectively: (12) δ p q = α · l p q ︸ δ ‾ p q + ( u q − u p ) ︸ δ ^ p q ρ p q = − l p q Ω ‾ ︸ ρ ‾ p q + ( − R p ω p − R q ω q ) ︸ ρ ^ p q , where l p q = R p + R q is the length of the branch vector l pq . Substituting the decompositions in Eq. (12) into Eq. (10), the total relative displacement Δ pq at the contact between particles p and q can be written as (13) Δ p q = ɛ · l p q + ( u q − u p ) + ( − R p ω p − R q ω q ) t p q , where the infinitesimal strain tensor ε is defined as the symmetric part of the displacement gradient tensor α , i.e. ε i j = ( α i j + α j i ) / 2 , and the relation ( α − ɛ ) · l p q = l p q Ω ‾ t p q has been used. The first term on the right-hand side of Eq. (13) represents the contribution of the imposed mean field to the contact deformation, whereas the second and third terms correspond to the fluctuation contributions associated with δ ^ p q and ρ ^ p q as defined in Eq. (12). 2.4 Micromechanical expressions for stress and strain tensors The micromechanical expression for the average stress tensor, in terms of contact force f c and branch vector l c (Drescher and de Josselin de Jong, 1972; Kruyt and Rothenburg, 1996; Weber, 1966), is (14) σ i j = 1 A ∑ c ∈ C f i c l j c , where the summation is over contacts c in the set of contacts C and A is the area (in the two-dimensional case considered here) occupied by the granular system (including both particles and voids). The micromechanical expression for the average displacement gradient tensor α is given in terms of the deformation due to particle displacements δ c at contacts and polygon vectors h c associated with contacts. The polygon vector h c is obtained by counter-clockwise rotation over 90∘ of the vector g c that connects the centres of the two polygons (or voids) associated with contact c, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Note that the polygon vector h c is not necessarily parallel to the branch vector l c . The expression for the average displacement gradient tensor (Kruyt and Rothenburg, 1996) is (15) α i j = 1 A ∑ c ∈ C δ i c h j c , where the summation is over contacts c in the set of contacts C and A is the area (in the two-dimensional case considered here) occupied by the system (including both particles and voids). Note that this expression for the average displacement gradient does not involve contributions due to particle rotations. The strain tensor ε equals the symmetric part of the displacement gradient tensor α . The branch vectors l c and the polygon vectors h c satisfy the geometrical relationship (16) I i j = 1 A ∑ c ∈ C l i c h j c , where I is the identity tensor. Due to the geometrical relationship Eq. (16), it follows from Eq. (12) that the contribution of particle displacement fluctuations to the relative displacements at contacts, δ ^ , satisfies (17) 0 = 1 A ∑ c ∈ C δ ^ i c h j c . In the presence of a spatially uniform macroscopic deformation, the contact-scale quantities, such as the contact force f c and the relative displacement Δ c , exhibit a dependence on the contact orientation θ (Bathurst and Rothenburg, 1988a; Rothenburg and Bathurst, 1989). Therefore, it is meaningful to group contacts with similar orientations and to determine averages for such groups of contacts. These group-averages for a contact quantity ψc are denoted by ψ ‾ ( θ ) . In terms of group-averages, the micromechanical expressions for the stress and the displacement gradient tensors become (18) σ i j = m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) f i l j ‾ ( θ ) d θ ≅ m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) f i ‾ ( θ ) l j ‾ ( θ ) d θ , (19) α i j = m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) δ i h j ‾ ( θ ) d θ ≅ m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) δ i ‾ ( θ ) h j ‾ ( θ ) d θ . In Eqs. (18) and (19), mA denotes the contact density defined by Eq. (1). The approximate equalities in the second expressions follow from the assumptions that the contact force and branch vector are statistically independent, and that the relative displacement and the polygon vector are statistically independent. The accuracy of Eq. (19) has been evaluated by Kruyt and Rothenburg (2019). By substituting the average relative displacement δ ‾ i with the fluctuation δ ^ i , the corresponding displacement gradient induced by the fluctuation α ^ i j can be obtained. The accuracy of the corresponding expression for α ^ i j is discussed in Section 3.3. 2.5 Constitutive relation at interparticle contact The relationship between (increments) of force and (increments) of relative displacement is described by the contact constitutive relation. For a linear elastic contact with springs acting in the normal and tangential directions, this relationship is written as (20) f p q = S p q · Δ p q S p q = k t I + ( k n − k t ) n p q n p q , where S pq is the symmetric contact stiffness tensor, and k n and k t are the (constant) normal and tangential elastic stiffnesses at the contact, respectively. The stiffness ratio λ is defined as (21) λ ≡ k t k n . 2.6 Homogenisation In the homogenisation framework (see Fig. 3 ) proposed by Cambou et al. (2000, 1995), a micromechanically based constitutive relation at the continuum, macro-scale involves a so-called (kinematic) “localisation operator” that expresses (group-averaged) relative displacements at the micro-scale of contacts in terms of the macro-scale strain tensor. By employing the micro-scale contact constitutive relation, the (group-averaged) forces at the contact level are obtained. Using the micromechanical averaging expression Eq. (14), the continuum, macro-scale stress tensor is obtained. For a localisation operator that relates macro-scale strain to micro-scale relative displacements, it is important that the localisation operator be consistent, such that the resulting relative displacements satisfy the micromechanical strain expression, Eq. (15). Such a consistency is not automatically guaranteed. It is shown in Section 3.1 that the pair-fluctuation theory of Jenkins et al. (2005) does not satisfy this consistency requirement. Therefore, a self-consistent pair-fluctuation theory is developed in this study. 2.7 Mean-field theory The bulk modulus K and the shear modulus G can be obtained from the stress-strain responses of granular materials subjected to isotropic compression and pure shear loading paths, respectively. These moduli are defined through the macroscopic stress-strain relationships for each loading condition, given as (22) σ 11 + σ 22 = 2 K ( ε 11 + ε 22 ) σ 11 − σ 22 = 2 G ( ε 11 − ε 22 ) , where σ 11 and σ 22 are the normal stress components, and ε11 and ε22 represent the corresponding strain components. The strain states for the isotropic compression and pure shear loading paths are expressed as (23) Compression : α = ε 0 [ 1 0 0 1 ] Pure shear : α = ε 0 [ 1 0 0 − 1 ] . In these expressions, α denotes the displacement gradient associated with each loading condition, with ε0 specifying the applied strain amplitude. With the prescribed loading paths, the relative displacements between contacting particles can be obtained using the kinematic localisation operator in the mean-field theory (Bathurst and Rothenburg, 1988a; Chang et al., 1990). In this approach, the displacement and rotation fluctuations are assumed to vanish, i.e. u p = 0 and ω p = 0 for all particles p in the granular assembly. As a consequence, the group-averaged relative displacement and relative rotation at contacts with orientation θ reduce to their mean-field parts and are given by (24) δ ‾ ( θ ) = α · l ‾ ( θ ) ρ ‾ = − 2 R ‾ Ω ‾ , where l ‾ ( θ ) is the average of the branch vectors associated with contacts of orientation θ, and R ‾ is the average particle radius. For the loading paths in Eq. (23), the mean-field rotation term is ρ ‾ = 0 . By substituting the mean-field kinematic relations (Eq. (24)) into the total relative displacement (Eq. (13)), and subsequently into the contact constitutive relation (Eq. (20)), the group-averaged contact force f ‾ ( θ ) is obtained. The corresponding macroscopic stress is then evaluated using the micromechanical stress expression (Eq. (18)). With the macroscopic stress and the prescribed loading paths (Eq. (23)), the mean-field bulk modulus K ε and shear modulus G ε follow directly from the macroscopic stress-strain relations (Eq. (22)) as (25) K ε k n = m A 4 l 0 2 ‾ G ε k n = m A 8 l 0 2 ‾ ( 1 + λ ) , where mA is the contact density defined in Eq. (1) and l 0 2 ‾ is the average (over contacts) of the squared length of the branch vector. In a more general framework, the fluctuations δ ^ can be expressed by (truncated) Fourier series (Kruyt and Rothenburg, 1998; 2001) as (26) Compression : δ ^ n ε 0 l 0 = a ^ 0 δ ^ t ε 0 l 0 = 0 ρ ^ ε 0 l 0 = 0 Pure shear : δ ^ n ε 0 l 0 = a ^ n cos 2 θ δ ^ t ε 0 l 0 = a ^ t sin 2 θ ρ ^ ε 0 l 0 = a ^ ρ sin 2 θ where δ ^ n and δ ^ t are the normal and tangential components of δ ^ , respectively. An example of the relative displacement fluctuations obtained from DEM simulations is presented in Section 4.3. According to DEM simulations, under isotropic compression, a ^ 0 is negative, indicating that the actual normal relative displacements are smaller than those predicted by the mean displacement field. The magnitude of this reduction increases for granular assemblies with lower contact stiffness ratios and lower coordination numbers. Under pure shear loading, a ^ n , a ^ t , and a ^ ρ vary with the contact stiffness ratio λ and the coordination number Z. In general, a ^ n 0 , while for dense granular assemblies with larger contact stiffness ratios, the tangential fluctuation becomes positive, a ^ t > 0 . With Eqs. (13), (18), (20) and (26), the corresponding stress tensors give the (effective) bulk and shear moduli that involve the fluctuations a ^ 0 , a ^ n , a ^ t and a ^ ρ (27) K k n = K ε k n + m A l 0 2 ‾ 4 a ^ 0 = m A l 0 2 ‾ 4 ( 1 + a ^ 0 ) G k n = G ε k n + m A l 0 2 ‾ 8 [ a ^ n − λ ( a ^ t + a ^ ρ ) ] = m A l 0 2 ‾ 8 [ 1 + a ^ n + λ ( 1 − a ^ t − a ^ ρ ) ] . In Eqs. (25) and (27), the average of the squared length of the branch vector, l 0 2 ‾ , is involved. Under the assumption that the coordination number of a particle is statistically independent of its radius, this quantity can be expressed as l 0 2 ‾ = l 0 ‾ 2 + 2 σ R 2 , where σR denotes the standard deviation of the particle radius distribution. A detailed analysis of this relation is given by Kruyt and Rothenburg (2001). 3 Self-consistent pair-fluctuation theory The self-consistent pair-fluctuation theory is derived following the procedure outlined in Fig. 3, by first linking the macroscopic strain tensor to interparticle relative displacements through the kinematic localisation operator, then applying the constitutive relation at interparticle contacts, and finally averaging the resulting contact forces to obtain the macroscopic stress tensor. In this multiscale approach, a key challenge lies in deriving the kinematic localisation operator, which must satisfy the kinematic consistency condition given by Eqs. (15) and (17). In Section 3.1, the pair-fluctuation framework is introduced first. To develop a self-consistent pair-fluctuation theory, the kinematic consistency condition is enforced by introducing additional variables, as discussed in Section 3.2 and 3.3. The localisation operator is then derived from the equilibrium and kinematic consistency conditions for small sub-assemblies. Finally, the effective elastic moduli are calculated using the derived localisation operator, as presented in Section 3.4. 3.1 Kinematics In the pair-fluctuation theory of Jenkins et al. (2005), a contact is considered between two particles p and q, as shown in Fig. 4 . The neighbours of particle p (excluding particle q), denoted as particles r ∈ Cp ∖{q}, are assumed to move according to the mean-field deformation. Similarly, the neighbours of particle q (excluding particle p), denoted as particles s ∈ Cq ∖{p}, are also assumed to move according to the mean-field deformation. Thus, for these particles, the fluctuations in displacements and rotations are equal to zero, (28) u r = 0 ω r = 0 ; r ∈ C p ∖ { q } u s = 0 ω s = 0 ; s ∈ C q ∖ { p } The displacement fluctuations u p and u q , and the rotation fluctuations ωp and ωq of particles p and q are determined from the force and moment equilibrium equations for particles p and q. The stress tensor is then obtained from the relative displacement at the contact through the contact constitutive relation. In the pair-fluctuation theory of Jenkins et al. (2005), the stiffnesses at all contacts are equal to k n and k t. However, as shown in Section 4, this results in an inconsistency of the relative displacement at the contacts with the overall displacement gradient, i.e. the kinematic relation Eq. (15) is not satisfied; hence, the localisation operator is not guaranteed to satisfy the kinematic consistency condition. To address this inconsistency and improve the accuracy of the predicted elastic moduli of granular assemblies, a self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory is developed in this study. The mean-field deformation assumption, as described in Eq. (28), does not accurately reflect the actual displacement field of the neighbouring particles, as it neglects the fluctuations that occur in the actual particle motion. To overcome this limitation, self-consistent (SC) contact stiffnesses, denoted as k n * and k t * , are introduced for the neighbouring contacts. These SC stiffnesses serve the purpose to neutralise the discrepancies between the motion under the mean field assumption and the actual motion of the neighbouring particles, and also to provide additional degrees of freedom to the system so that the kinematic consistency can be ensured. In this framework, a “central” reference contact between particles p and q is treated as an inclusion (as described in the context of heterogeneous materials; Torquato et al. (2002)), characterised by stiffnesses k n and k t. This reference contact is surrounded by neighbouring contacts, which are assumed to have the SC stiffnesses k n * and k t * , potentially softer than the reference stiffnesses. Here, the SC stiffnesses are assumed to have the same contact stiffness ratio as the reference contact, i.e., (29) k t * k n * = k t k n = λ . The SC stiffnesses are incorporated into the SC stiffness matrix S *, which has the same form as Eq. (20). The displacement and rotation fluctuations, along with the self-consistent stiffnesses k n * and k t * , are solved in a coupled manner through the equilibrium equations and the kinematic consistency condition (as expressed in Eq. (17)). By introducing these SC stiffnesses, the SC-PF theory reduces the discrepancy between the mean-field deformation assumption and the actual particle motion, and further improves the prediction of the elastic moduli by more accurately capturing the true kinematics of the granular assembly. 3.2 Equilibrium conditions The derivation of the equilibrium equations follows a procedure similar to that presented in Duan et al. (2025). Duan et al. (2025) formulated the equilibrium conditions for an individual particle, while the present study derives the equilibrium equations for a pair of particles in contact. The displacement fluctuations u p and u q , along with the rotation fluctuations ωp and ωq of particles p and q, are determined from the force and moment equilibrium equations, Eqs. (8) and (9), for particles p and q. These equations can be written in terms of the displacement and rotation fluctuations as (where Eqs. (13), (20) and (28) have been used) (30) S p q · ( δ ^ p q + ρ ^ p q t p q ) − ∑ r ∈ C p ∖ { q } S * p r · ( u p + R p ω p t p r ) = − S p q · ɛ · l p q − ∑ r ∈ C p ∖ { q } S * p r · ɛ · l p r S q p · ( δ ^ q p + ρ ^ q p t q p ) − ∑ s ∈ C q ∖ { p } S * q s · ( u q + R q ω q t q s ) = − S q p · ɛ · l q p − ∑ s ∈ C q ∖ { p } S * q s · ɛ · l q s Eq. (30) represents the force equilibrium conditions for two contacting particles, p and q, where the terms on the left-hand side correspond to the forces arising from the relative displacement fluctuations, while those on the right-hand side represent the forces due to mean-field deformation, with a negative sign due to the rearrangement of the expressions. Analogously, using Eqs. (9), (13), (20) and (28), the moment equilibrium conditions for particles p and q can be expressed as (31) t p q · S p q · ( δ ^ p q + ρ ^ p q t p q ) − ∑ r ∈ C p ∖ { q } t p r · S * p r · ( u p + R p ω p t p r ) = − t p q · S p q · ɛ · l p q − ∑ r ∈ C p ∖ { q } t p r · S * p r · ɛ · l p r t q p · S q p · ( δ ^ q p + ρ ^ q p t q p ) − ∑ s ∈ C q ∖ { p } t q s · S * q s · ( u q + R q ω q t q s ) = − t q p · S q p · ɛ · l q p − ∑ s ∈ C q ∖ { p } t q s · S * q s · ɛ · l q s The equilibrium conditions Eqs. (30) and (31) contain terms of the type ∑ r ∈ C p ∖ { q } ψ p r that involve a summation over contacts with a specified excluded contact. In order to facilitate analytical developments, these sums are converted to integrals that involve the conditional contact distribution e(β | θ) (defined in Eq. (2)) (32) ∑ r ∈ C p ∖ { q } ψ p r → 〈 ψ 〉 θ p q ∑ s ∈ C q ∖ { p } ψ q s → 〈 ψ 〉 θ q p . The argument θpq in 〈 ψ 〉 θ p q signifies the orientation of the excluded contact in the summation. The definition of the averaging operator ⟨ψ⟩ θ in Eq. (32), which depends on contact orientation θ, for a contact quantity ψc is given in terms of the group-average ψ ‾ by (33) 〈 ψ 〉 θ = ( Z − 1 ) ∫ − π + π e ( β | θ ) ψ ‾ ( β ) d β . Using the equivalence property in Eq. (3), it follows that the averaging operator ⟨ψ⟩ θ defined in Eq. (33) satisfies (34) 〈 ψ 〉 θ + π = ± 〈 ψ 〉 θ if ψ ( β + π ) = ± ψ ( β ) . By combining the definition in Eq. (33) with Eq. (4) that is valid for isotropic granular systems, the average ⟨ψ⟩ θ can also be expressed as (with the substitution of β = β ′ + θ ) (35) 〈 ψ 〉 θ = ( Z − 1 ) ∫ − π + π e iso ( β ′ ) ψ ‾ ( θ + β ′ ) d β ′ , where e iso is the conditional contact orientation distribution for isotropic systems defined in Eq. (5). With the transformation of Eq. (32) to a continuous formulation, the force equilibrium equations (Eq. (30)) for particles p and q become (36) S p q · ( δ ^ p q + ρ ^ p q t p q ) − 〈 S * 〉 θ p q · u p − 〈 S * · t 〉 θ p q ( R p ω p ) = − S p q · ɛ · l p q − 〈 S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ p q S q p · ( δ ^ q p + ρ ^ q p t q p ) − 〈 S * 〉 θ q p · u q − 〈 S * · t 〉 θ q p ( R q ω q ) = − S q p · ɛ · l q p − 〈 S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ q p Similarly, the moment equilibrium equations (Eq. (31)) for particles p and q are expressed as (37) t p q · S p q · ( δ ^ p q + ρ ^ p q t p q ) − 〈 t · S * 〉 θ p q · u p − 〈 t · S * · t 〉 θ p q ( R p ω p ) = − t p q · S p q · ɛ · l p q − 〈 t · S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ p q t q p · S q p · ( δ ^ q p + ρ ^ q p t q p ) − 〈 t · S * 〉 θ q p · u q − 〈 t · S * · t 〉 θ q p ( R q ω q ) = − t q p · S q p · ɛ · l q p − 〈 t · S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ q p By taking the difference of the force equilibrium equations (Eq. (36)) for particles p and q, it follows that (using n q p = − n p q , t q p = − t p q , S q p = S p q , δ ^ q p = − δ ^ p q , ρ q p = ρ p q , θ q p = θ p q + π and Eqs. (13) and (34)) (38) 2 S p q · ( δ ^ p q + ρ ^ p q t p q ) + 〈 S * 〉 θ p q · δ ^ p q + 〈 S * · t 〉 θ p q ρ ^ p q = − 2 S p q · ɛ · l p q − 2 〈 S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ p q By taking the sum of the moment equilibrium equations (Eq. (37)) for particles p and q, it follows that (39) 2 t p q · S p q · ( δ ^ p q + ρ ^ p q t p q ) + 〈 t · S * 〉 θ p q · δ ^ p q + 〈 t · S * · t 〉 θ p q ρ ^ p q = − 2 t p q · S p q · ɛ · l p q − 2 〈 t · S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ p q Eqs. (38) and (39) describe the force and moment equilibrium equations for a pair of contacting particles, p and q, with a contact orientation θ. By group-averaging Eqs. (38) and (39) over all contacting particle pairs that share similar orientations, the overall equilibrium equations of the system are derived as (40) 2 S · ( δ ^ ‾ + ρ ^ ‾ t ) + 〈 S * 〉 θ · δ ^ ‾ + 〈 S * · t 〉 θ ρ ^ ‾ = − 2 S · ɛ · l − 2 〈 S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ 2 t · S · ( δ ^ ‾ + ρ ^ ‾ t ) + 〈 t · S * 〉 θ · δ ^ ‾ + 〈 t · S * · t 〉 θ ρ ^ ‾ = − 2 t · S · ɛ · l − 2 〈 t · S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ where the unknowns are the group-averaged relative displacement due to fluctuations in the displacements of particle centres and in the rotations, δ ^ ‾ ( θ ) and ρ ^ ‾ ( θ ) , respectively. Note that in these equations, S = S ( θ ) , l = l ( θ ) and t = t ( θ ) are functions of contact orientation θ when present outside of the averaging operator ⟨ · ⟩ θ . The group-averaged relative displacement due to displacement fluctuations, δ ^ ‾ , can be decomposed into normal and tangential components, denoted by δ ^ ‾ n and δ ^ ‾ t , respectively: (41) δ ^ ‾ ( θ ) = δ ^ ‾ n ( θ ) n ( θ ) + δ ^ ‾ t ( θ ) t ( θ ) . With Eq. (41), the force equilibrium conditions in Eq. (40) can be expressed in normal and tangential directions. Hence, Eq. (40) forms a system of linear equations in unknowns δ ^ ‾ n ( θ ) , δ ^ ‾ t ( θ ) and ρ ^ ‾ ( θ ) , given by (42) [ n · ( 2 S + 〈 S * 〉 θ ) · n n · ( 2 S + 〈 S * 〉 θ ) · t n · ( 2 S · t + 〈 S * · t 〉 θ ) t · ( 2 S + 〈 S * 〉 θ ) · n t · ( 2 S + 〈 S * 〉 θ ) · t t · ( 2 S · t + 〈 S * · t 〉 θ ) n · ( 2 S · t + 〈 S * · t 〉 θ ) t · ( 2 S · t + 〈 S * · t 〉 θ ) 2 t · S · t + 〈 t · S * · t 〉 θ ] [ δ ^ ‾ n δ ^ ‾ t ρ ^ ‾ ] = [ − 2 n · ( S · ɛ · l + 〈 S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ ) − 2 t · ( S · ɛ · l + 〈 S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ ) − 2 ( t · S · ɛ · l + 〈 t · S * · ɛ · l 〉 θ ) ] The 3 × 3 matrix on the left-hand side of Eq. (42) represents the stiffness associated with the fluctuations in the relative displacement, δ ^ ‾ n ( θ ) , δ ^ ‾ t ( θ ) , and ρ ^ ‾ ( θ ) . The terms on the right-hand side of Eq. (42) form a 3 × 1 vector, representing the normal and tangential forces and moment corresponding to mean-field deformation, which accounts for the loading path through the strain tensor ε . Throughout the small strain-increment regime considered here, the contact network is assumed to be fixed: contacts are treated as bonded, i.e. neither contact creation nor contact disruption is permitted. For a prescribed macroscopic strain ε , solving Eq. (42) for δ ^ ‾ n ( θ ) , δ ^ ‾ t ( θ ) , and ρ ^ ‾ ( θ ) therefore defines a localisation operator in the multiscale homogenisation framework of Cambou et al. (1995). Using Eqs. (4), (6), (7) and (35), it follows after some algebra that the 3 × 3 stiffness matrix H on the left-hand side of Eq. (42) can be written as (43) H = [ 2 k n + μ 2 k n * + ( μ 0 − μ 2 ) k t * 0 0 0 2 k t + ( μ 0 − μ 2 ) k n * + μ 2 k t * 2 k t + μ 1 k t * 0 2 k t + μ 1 k t * 2 k t + μ 0 k t * ] = [ H 11 0 0 0 H 22 H 23 0 H 32 H 33 ] where μm are moments of the probability density function e iso(β) defined in Eq. (7). These moments arise in the expression for the stiffness matrix H through the averaging operator ⟨ · ⟩ θ defined in Eq. (35), as illustrated in the representative derivation in Appendix A. From Eq. (43), it is shown that the stiffness matrix is determined solely by the moments μm and the contact stiffnesses k n, k t, k n * , and k t * . By substituting the fluctuations in terms of the Fourier series (Eq. (26)) into Eq. (42), one linear equilibrium equation for the isotropic compression loading path and three for the pure shear loading path (as shown in Eq. (23)) are derived for the four fluctuation amplitudes, i.e., a ^ 0 , a ^ n , a ^ t , and a ^ ρ . For isotropic compression, where δ ^ ‾ t , ρ ^ ‾ , and the last two elements of the right-hand side vector in Eq. (42) are zero, Eq. (42) can be rewritten as (44) [ H 11 ] [ a ^ 0 ] = [ − 2 k n − 2 μ 1 k n * ] . For pure shear, Eq. (42) can be rewritten as three linear equations (45) [ H 11 0 0 0 H 22 H 23 0 H 32 H 33 ] [ a ^ n a ^ t a ^ ρ ] = [ − 2 k n + ( 2 μ 1 − 4 μ 3 ) k n * + ( − 4 μ 1 + 4 μ 3 ) k t * 2 k t + ( 4 μ 1 − 4 μ 3 ) k n * + ( − 2 μ 1 + 4 μ 3 ) k t * 2 k t + ( − 2 μ 0 + 4 μ 2 ) k t * ] . In Eqs. (44) and (45), the SC stiffnesses k n * and k t * remain to be determined. In the next subsection, the kinematic consistency condition Eq. (17) is applied, which introduces an additional equation to solve Eqs. (44) and (45). Solving the equilibrium conditions Eqs. (44) and (45) yields the fluctuations a ^ 0 , a ^ n , a ^ t , and a ^ ρ , and thus determines the corresponding kinematic fields. These solutions depend on the self-consistent contact stiffnesses k n * and k t * , which are determined in the following. 3.3 Kinematic consistency In multiscale constitutive modelling of granular materials, it is crucial that the localisation operator satisfies the kinematic consistency condition. Specifically, the consistency condition requires that the macroscopic strain, obtained by averaging the relative displacements (such as in Eq. (15) for the total relative displacement and Eq. (17) for the fluctuations), be equal to the original macroscopic strain that has been used in the localisation process (for example, solutions of Eq. (42)). For a specified loading path in Eq. (23), δ ^ ‾ n ( θ ) , δ ^ ‾ t ( θ ) , and ρ ^ ‾ ( θ ) are determined from Eq. (42). The consistency of the obtained relative displacements δ ^ ‾ n ( θ ) , δ ^ ‾ t ( θ ) due to particle displacements with the imposed average displacement gradient needs to be investigated. For kinematic consistency, following Eq. (17), the displacement gradient arising from the fluctuations in particle displacements should be zero (Kruyt and Rothenburg, 2001; 2002): (46) α ^ i j = m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) δ ^ i h j ‾ d θ = 0 . Eq. (46) contains the average of the product of the relative displacement fluctuations δ ^ i and the polygon vectors hj (see Fig. 2), i.e. δ ^ i h j ‾ , which cannot be evaluated analytically. In contrast, the integral of the product of the individual averages of δ ^ i and hj, i.e., δ ^ i ‾ h ‾ j , can be related to the relative displacement fluctuations under isotropic compression and pure shear loading. Under the pure shear loading path (see Eq. (26)), this relationship takes the form of (47) m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) δ ^ i ‾ h ‾ j d θ = m A ε 0 l 0 h 0 4 ( a ^ n − a ^ t ) S i j , where Sij are the components of S = [ 1 0 0 − 1 ] . If the relative displacement fluctuations δ ^ and the polygon vectors h were statistically independent, then the identity δ ^ i h j ‾ = δ ^ i ‾ h ‾ j would hold, and the fluctuation amplitudes should consequently satisfy a ^ n − a ^ t = 0 (from Eqs. (46) and (47)). However, DEM results in this study as well as the analysis of Kruyt and Rothenburg (2019) show that this independence assumption does not hold precisely. To quantify the correlation between the relative displacement fluctuations δ ^ and the polygon vectors h , the covariance δ ^ i h j ‾ − δ ^ i ‾ h ‾ j between these quantities is evaluated numerically. Based on this covariance, the correlation factor C S (corresponding to the pure shear loading path) is defined as (48) m A ∫ − π + π E ( θ ) ( δ ^ i h j ‾ − δ ^ i ‾ h ‾ j ) d θ = m A ε 0 l 0 h 0 4 C S S i j , Hence, C S provides a scalar measure of the coupling between the kinematic fluctuations and the local geometric structure. In the special case where the two quantities are uncorrelated, the covariance vanishes and thus C S = 0 . Since the consistency condition Eq. (46) must hold, the left-hand side of Eq. (48) reduces to the negative of the left-hand side of Eq. (47). Consequently, the kinematic consistency condition for pure shear loading requires that the right-hand sides of Eq. (47) and Eq. (48) be opposite in sign. This yields (49) a ^ n − a ^ t = − C S . As shown by the DEM data in Fig. 5 a, the correlation factor C S is non-zero under pure shear loading. Moreover, C S depends on both the coordination number Z and the contact stiffness ratio λ. In denser granular assemblies, the relative displacement fluctuations δ ^ and the polygon vectors h exhibit a weak correlation. As the contact stiffness ratio λ increases, C S decreases, and its rate of variation gradually diminishes as λ approaches 1. Based on these observations, an expression for the correlation factor C S as a function of the coordination number Z and the stiffness ratio λ is proposed in an uncoupled manner, i.e. (50) C S ( Z , λ ) = C S Z ( Z ) + C S λ ( λ ) . The corresponding expressions, fitted from DEM simulation results, are C S Z ( Z ) = − 0.16 Z + 0.87 and C S λ ( λ ) = e − 4.97 λ − 2.26 , where C S Z is linearly dependent on Z, and C S λ is exponentially dependent on λ, as observed from DEM simulations. These fitted results of C S fit are represented by the blue surface in Fig. 5b. This expression achieves a coefficient of determination of R 2 = 0.97 , demonstrating excellent agreement with the numerical data. Appendix F further validates the fitted relation for C S in Eq. (50) against additional DEM simulations not used in the fitting procedure. 3.4 Elastic moduli The resulting five equations, including the stiffness ratio relation (Eq. (29)) between k n * and k t * , together with the equilibrium (Eq. (45)) and kinematic consistency conditions (Eq. (49)) under the pure shear loading path, are used to determine five unknowns: the displacement fluctuation amplitudes a ^ n , a ^ t , and a ^ ρ , as well as the self-consistent (SC) stiffnesses k n * and k t * . The detailed solving procedure for the SC stiffnesses k n * and k t * is presented in Appendix B. The calculation procedure for the effective elastic bulk and shear moduli is summarised as follows: 1) The self-consistent (SC) normal contact stiffness k n * is first obtained by solving the quadratic equation given in Eq. (B.2) (see Appendix B). The corresponding SC shear stiffness is then determined from the stiffness-ratio relation k t * = λ k n * . 2) Substituting k n * and k t * into the force and moment equilibrium equations for isotropic compression (Eq. (44)) and pure shear (Eq. (45)), the relative displacement fluctuations a ^ 0 , a ^ n , a ^ t , and a ^ ρ are obtained. 3) Finally, by substituting these fluctuations into Eq. (27), the effective elastic bulk and shear moduli, K and G, are obtained. Additionally, the effective elastic moduli derived under the identical contact stiffness condition used in Jenkins et al. (2005) (which assumes all contacts to have identical stiffnesses k n and k t) can be obtained by directly solving the displacement fluctuations from Eqs. (44) and (45) with k n * = k n and k t * = k t . 4 Verification of the analytical self-consistent theory In this section, two-dimensional DEM simulations under isotropic compression and pure shear loading paths are performed using the open-source code YADE (Šmilauer et al., 2021). Data on the conditional contact distribution, particle displacement and rotation, and the macroscopic stress-strain response of the granular assemblies are collected. The effective elastic moduli, computed from the DEM simulation data, the mean-field theory, the pair-fluctuation theory (with an identical contact stiffness condition) formulated by Jenkins et al. (2005) (referred to as the IS-PF approach, with “IS” denoting “identical stiffness”), and the self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory, are then compared. The SC stiffnesses k n * and k t * corresponding to the SC-PF theory are reported in Appendix C. The assessment of kinematic consistency in the SC-PF and IS-PF theories is presented in Appendix D. 4.1 DEM simulations Three isotropic granular assemblies with coordination numbers of Z = 3.46 , 4.02, and 4.48 were considered (these values were obtained after removal of rattlers, i.e., particles with fewer than two contacts). The assemblies were generated through isotropic compression under different non-dimensional pressures of p / k n = 3.8 × 10 − 5 , 5.0 × 10 − 4 , and 9.0 × 10 − 3 , and friction angles of 15∘, 1∘, and 0.1∘, respectively. Each assembly consisted of 50,000 spherical particles, whose radii followed a log-normal distribution with a coefficient of variation of 0.25. As YADE simulates three-dimensional systems, the two-dimensional simulations were achieved by assigning all particle centres to lie in a plane. Periodic boundary conditions were applied in all DEM simulations to minimise boundary effects. The size of the square periodic box is approximately 220 particle diameters in length. Both isotropic compression and pure shear loading paths (defined in Eq. (23), with a strain magnitude of ε 0 = 10 − 4 ) were applied to the three granular assemblies with different coordination numbers. The linear elastic contact constitutive relation described in Eq. (20) was adopted. All contacts shared a uniform normal stiffness k n, regardless of particle size, whereas the tangential stiffness k t was governed by the contact stiffness ratio λ (defined in Eq. (21)). Eight stiffness ratios λ were uniformly varied between 0.125 and 1.0 for each assembly. Consequently, a total of 2 × 3 × 8 = 48 simulations were performed, corresponding to two loading paths, three coordination numbers, and eight stiffness ratios. During both compression and pure shear loading, the topology of the contact network was fixed by setting the contacts as “bonded”, i.e., contact creation and breakage were not allowed. 4.2 Conditional contact distribution The conditional contact orientation distribution e iso(β) (defined in Eq. (5)) for three isotropic granular assemblies with coordination numbers Z = 3.46 , 4.02, and 4.48 is shown in Fig. 6 a. The distribution clearly exhibits the spherical exclusion effect, characterised by e iso ( β ) = e ( θ + β ∣ θ ) ≈ 0 for − 60 ∘ 0, d 1 > 0 and d 0 < 0, and the discriminant Δ ( D ) = d 1 2 − 4 d 2 d 0 is positive. Consequently, the quadratic equation admits a real and positive solution for the SC normal contact stiffness k n * , given by (B.3) k n * = − d 1 + d 1 2 − 4 d 2 d 0 2 d 2 . The other root, ( − d 1 − d 1 2 − 4 d 2 d 0 ) / ( 2 d 2 ) , is non-physical (negative) and therefore disregarded. The SC shear stiffness is then obtained from the stiffness-ratio relation Eq. (29), i.e. k t * = λ k n * . The SC stiffnesses k n * and k t * , solved with respect to the DEM simulations described in Section 4.1, are shown in Appendix C. Substituting the obtained values of k n * and k t * into Eq. (44) for isotropic compression and Eq. (45) for pure shear, the fluctuations a ^ 0 , a ^ n , a ^ t , and a ^ ρ are determined. By further substituting these fluctuations into the elastic moduli expressions in Eq. (27), the effective bulk and shear moduli, K and G, are obtained for granular assemblies with different coordination numbers Z and stiffness ratios λ. Additionally, using the obtained SC stiffnesses k n * and k t * , together with the moments μm of granular assemblies with coordination numbers Z = 3.46 , 4.02, and 4.48, the numerical stability of the pure-shear equilibrium equations (Eq. (45)) and the solvability of the quadratic equation for k n * (Eq. (B.1)) can be assessed. The condition number of the coefficient matrix H in Eq. (45), defined by κ ( H ) = ∥ H ∥ · ∥ H − 1 ∥ , varies between 1.62 and 7.48. The smallest value occurs for the denser assembly with the highest stiffness ratio ( Z = 4.48 , λ = 1 ), while the largest corresponds to the loosest assembly with the lowest stiffness ratio ( Z = 3.46 , λ = 0.125 ). Similarly, the discriminant of Eq. (B.1), Δ ( D ) = d 1 2 − 4 d 2 d 0 , ranges from 2.8 to 9000, with the same trend: larger values for loose assemblies with small λ and smaller values for dense assemblies with large λ. These observations indicate that the system is numerically more stable for dense granular assemblies with higher contact stiffness ratios. Appendix C Self-consistent contact stiffnesses in SC-PF theory Fig. C.10 shows the SC normal and shear contact stiffnesses, k n * and k t * , obtained from the equilibrium equations (Eq. (45)) and the kinematic consistency condition (Eq. (49)). Both k n * and k t * are lower than the corresponding real contact stiffnesses, k n and k t. Moreover, for granular assemblies with higher coordination numbers and higher stiffness ratios, the SC stiffnesses approach the real stiffnesses more closely, consistent with the smaller displacement fluctuations observed in Fig. 8a. This behaviour corroborates the fundamental principle of the SC theory: the SC stiffnesses act to neutralise the discrepancies between the mean-field deformation and the actual particle motion, i.e., the displacement fluctuations. Since these fluctuations are predominantly negative, the SC stiffnesses are expected to be softer than the real contact stiffnesses. Fig. 10 The SC contact stiffnesses k n * and k t * solved from the equilibrium equations (Eq. (45)) and the kinematic consistency condition (Eq. (49)). Results are normalised by k n . The grey line in (b) indicates k t = λ k n as a reference. Fig. 10 dummy alt text Appendix D Assessment of kinematic consistency in SC-PF and IS-PF theories Fig. 11 Compliances of the SC-PF theory in this study and the IS-PF theory of Jenkins et al. (2005) with the kinematic self-consistency condition, represented by the relative deviation of the relative displacement fluctuations, a ^ n − a ^ t , from the reference value − C S obtained from DEM simulations. Fig. 11 dummy alt text The compliance of the self-consistent pair-fluctuation (SC-PF) theory developed in this study, and the identical-stiffness pair-fluctuation (IS-PF) theory of Jenkins et al. (2005), with respect to the kinematic consistency condition is assessed. According to Eq. (49), the fluctuation amplitudes predicted by the localisation operator must satisfy a ^ n − a ^ t = − C S . To evaluate this requirement, the values of a ^ n − a ^ t predicted by the SC-PF and IS-PF theories (shown in Fig. 8b and 8 c) are compared against the reference value − C S extracted from DEM simulations (Fig. 5a), with the comparison shown in Fig. D.11. The results demonstrate that the SC-PF theory exhibits substantially better compliance, with relative deviations below 8%. By incorporating the kinematic consistency condition into its derivation, the SC-PF theory achieves a markedly higher degree of kinematic consistency than the IS-PF theory. Fig. 12 Relative deviations in the predicted effective bulk and shear elastic moduli obtained from various theoretical approaches and from the moduli expression based on the relative displacement fluctuations (Eq. (27)), using the moduli directly extracted from DEM simulations (“DEM-Direct” in Fig. 9) as the reference data. Results are presented for granular assemblies with coordination numbers (a) Z = 3.46 , (b) Z = 4.02 , and (c) Z = 4.48 . Fig. 12 dummy alt text Appendix E Comparison of the elastic moduli derived from different theories The relative deviations between the theoretical predictions and the DEM results are shown in Fig. D.12, where the elastic moduli extracted directly from the stress-strain response obtained in DEM simulations are used as reference values. For the bulk modulus K, the deviations decrease with increasing coordination number Z and stiffness ratio λ, indicating improved accuracy in denser granular assemblies with a higher contact stiffness ratio. In contrast, the shear modulus G exhibits the lowest deviations at intermediate stiffness ratios (λ approximately from 0.25 to 0.5), rather than at the highest stiffness ratios. This behaviour stems from the fact that the mean-field prediction for the shear modulus increases linearly with the stiffness ratio λ, whereas the actual shear modulus tends to a plateau as λ → 1. Since the SC-PF and IS-PF theories retain a mean-field contribution, their predictions are also affected by this mismatch from the mean-field approximation at higher stiffness ratios. The expressions for the elastic moduli based on the relative displacement fluctuations (Eq. (27)) give the “DEM-Fluctuation” results, which agree with the “DEM-Direct” moduli to within 2%, thereby validating Eq. (27). For the shear modulus, all theoretical approaches overestimate the DEM values, with the SC-PF theory providing the closest predictions, followed by the IS-PF theory, and the mean-field theory performing the least accurately. The SC-PF theory consistently captures the shear modulus with high accuracy across all cases considered. For the bulk modulus, the mean-field and IS-PF theories overestimate the DEM results, whereas the SC-PF theory yields slightly lower values. Quantitatively, for the bulk modulus K, the proposed SC-PF theory reduces the relative deviations (mean values averaged over all considered coordination numbers and contact stiffness ratios) by 69% compared with mean-field predictions and by 22% compared with the IS-PF theory. For the shear modulus G, the corresponding reductions in relative deviation are 65% and 45%, respectively. Appendix F Validation of the fitted relation for C S and sensitivity analysis To evaluate the robustness of the fitted expression Eq. (50) for the correlation factor C S and to avoid validating the fitted relation against the same data used for its calibration, additional DEM simulations are used for validation. These simulations involve four granular assemblies with coordination numbers Z = 3.36 , 3.74, 4.26 and 4.73, which were not included in the fitting for C S (introduced in Section 3.3). The specimen preparation procedure and loading protocols are the same as those described in Section 4.1. Fig. 13 Comparison between the fitted correlation factor C S and the values extracted from DEM simulations. Each point represents one combination of coordination number Z and stiffness ratio λ . The calibration cases, with Z = 3.46 , 4.02, and 4.48, were used to fit the C S relation, while the validation cases, with Z = 3.36 , 3.74, 4.26 and 4.73, were not included in the fitting procedure. The corresponding RMSE values for the calibration and validation cases are 0.011 and 0.022, respectively. Fig. 13 dummy alt text Fig. 14 Validation of the SC-PF theoretical predictions for the effective bulk and shear moduli against the independent DEM validation set. Results are shown for the additional granular assemblies with coordination numbers (a) Z = 3.36 and (b) Z = 4.73 , confirming the robust predictive capability of the SC-PF theory for coordination numbers outside the calibrated range. Fig. 14 dummy alt text Fig. F.13 compares the values of C S predicted by the fitted expression with those directly extracted from the DEM validation data. The validation cases are distributed around the 1:1 reference line and follow the trend established by the calibration data. The root-mean-square error (RMSE) for the validation cases is 0.022, compared with 0.011 for the calibration data, indicating that the proposed expression for C S maintains consistent predictive accuracy for granular assemblies that were not used in the fitting procedure. To further validate the predicted elastic moduli for coordination numbers outside the calibrated range, Fig. F.14 shows the effective moduli predicted by the SC-PF theory using the fitted C S expression for the validation cases with Z = 3.36 and 4.73. The results for the two additional assemblies are consistent with those discussed in Section 3.4 for the calibration cases. The predicted moduli exhibit similar and continuous trends with coordination number Z and stiffness ratio λ, indicating that the SC-PF theory retains its predictive capability for the validation cases. Fig. 15 Sensitivity analysis illustrating the effect of perturbations ( ± 5 % and ± 10 % ) in the correlation factor C S on the predicted effective elastic moduli. Results of granular assemblies with coordination number Z = 3.46 and 4.48 are displayed. Fig. 15 dummy alt text The sensitivity of the predicted moduli to the fitted expression for C S is also examined. In Fig. F.15, perturbations of ± 5% and ± 10% have been applied to the fitted C S values, and the elastic moduli predicted by the SC-PF theory are computed. The normalised sensitivity of the bulk modulus is defined as S K = | Δ K / K 0 | / | Δ C S / C S 0 | , with K 0 and C S0 denoting the unperturbed values. The sensitivity SG is defined analogously for the shear modulus. For the loose assembly ( Z = 3.46 ), a perturbation of ± 10% in C S leads to maximum relative variations of 6.1% in K and 6.7% in G. The corresponding normalised sensitivities, averaged over different stiffness ratios λ, are 0.21 and 0.27, respectively. For the dense assembly ( Z = 4.48 ), smaller sensitivities are obtained, indicating that the predictions become less sensitive to the fitted relation for C S as the coordination number increases. The results in Fig. F.15 also show that the relative changes in the moduli are approximately proportional to the imposed perturbations in C S. These bounded sensitivities indicate that the SC-PF predictions are stable with respect to moderate perturbations in C S. The non-zero response of the moduli to C S also confirms that the correlation between the relative displacement fluctuations δ ^ and the polygon vectors h is a necessary closure relation in the SC-PF theory. title: A parametric study of slow dynamic nonlinear elasticity with comparisons to models
journal: Journal of the Mechanics and Physics of Solids
abstract: Several phenomenological models that aspire to quantitative description of anomalous nonlinear mesoscopic elasticity are reviewed and compared with laboratory measurements. This class of nonlinearity, best known perhaps for slow dynamics and aging, is seen widely in imperfectly consolidated granular solids but is not well understood. Typical slow dynamic tests show that a modest conditioning oscillatory "pump" strain depresses material stiffness, which then recovers like the logarithm of time after conditioning ceases. Several phenomenological models based on physical arguments have been proposed that predict the material stiffness response to arbitrary pump strain histories during conditioning and recovery. Approximate closed form and numerical solutions to the models are presented that predict the quantitative influence of three key pump parameters: the pump's strain amplitude, the pump's strain rate, and the pump’s duration. Laboratory measurements on Berea sandstone, concrete and a confined single aluminum bead find that slow dynamic responses are linear in pump strain and independent of pump frequency. Measurements also show that, after pump-off, stiffness recovers over times far longer than the pump duration. These observations and others are compared to model predictions. One of the considered models, based on a picture of fast brittle damage and slow healing, successfully matches all these behaviors.
