Section 3 employs a smoothing approximation that permits compact closed form expressions for δ C if oscillatory coefficients like p ( t ) are replaced by smoothed versions. Section 4 presents numerical integrations without smoothing. Conclusions are drawn for each model's stiffness loss and recovery and how these depend on E , ω , and D . 3 A smoothing approximation Each of the above differential equations for N ( t ) has a rapidly oscillating coefficient p ( t ) . If time resolutions of interest are cruder than 1 / ω , it may be useful to approximate the evolution of the N by replacing each such coefficient with its short-time-average. The averages of the pump function for each of the models are (7a) 〈 p 〉 = P = 2 π ν E ω n for Models 1 and 2 , (7b) P τ = 1 2 λ E 2 ( ω 2 + τ − 2 ) for Model 3 , (7c) P b = 2 π arccos ( ϵ b E ) for Models 4 and 5 , where n = 1 , 0 , and 1 / 2 for Models 1, 2a, and 2b, respectively. For Model 3, a subscript τ is included with P to emphasize its functional dependence, P τ = P ( τ ) . Similarly, for Models 4 and 5, P is a function of the bond’s rupture strength: P b = P ( ϵ b ) = 〈 H ( | ϵ | − ϵ b ) 〉 . For all b such that ϵ b > E , P b vanishes. Conversely, for b such that ϵ b 0 after the end of pumping, N recovers like d N d ξ = − N / τ for all models, so (15) N τ = N τ ( t = D ) e − ξ / τ ; ξ > 0 . Expressions for N τ ( t = D ) are given in Eqs. (8) – (10). Stiffness deficit during recovery δ C ( ξ ) is given by integrating Eq. 15 over τ for Models 1-3 (Eq. 2) or over τ and ϵ b for Models 4 and 5 (Eq. 5). Recall that N is a function of both ϵ b and τ for the latter (Eq. 10). Specifying times of interest ξ to be those that satisfy τ m i n ≪ ξ ≪ τ m a x , we obtain the following approximate expressions for δ C ( ξ ) : (16) δ C ( ξ ) = m [ γ + ln ( ξ τ m a x ) + E 1 ( ξ + d τ m a x ) ] for Models 1 , 2 , 3 , and 5 , where E 1 is the exponential integral: E 1 ( x ) ≡ ∫ x ∞ e x p ( − u ) d u / u , 3 3 Useful approximations are E 1 ( z ) ∼ − γ − ln ( z ) for z ≪ 1 , and ∼ exp ( − z ) / z ∼ 0 for z ≫ 1 . and (17) δ C ( ξ ) = m [ γ + ln ( ξ τ m a x ) ] for Model 4 . The effective durations d differ for each model: d = ( 1 + P ) D for Models 1 and 2, d = D for Model 3, and d = ( 1 + z 2 ) D for Model 5. For Models 1 and 2, because P ∝ E (the pump strain amplitude), P must be less than 1 so that m ∝ E , which has been established by previous work (TenCate et al., 2000; Yoritomo and Weaver, 2020a) and the experiments here (Section 5). This implies that d ∼ D for Models 1 and 2, whereas no such restriction is placed on d for Model 5 (i.e., z can be large). For Model 3, an additional assumption that ξ ω ≫ 1 has been invoked to obtain Eq. (16). For all models, derivations and more general expressions for δ C ( ξ ) are given in Appendix A. For all models except 4, the stiffness deficit δ C is log-linear for times ξ well before d (where the E 1 term in Eq. 16 is approximately independent of ξ ). Extrapolation of this log-linear behavior to full recovery δ C = 0 occurs at ξ = t e x , where (18) t ex = τ m a x exp [ − γ − E 1 ( d τ m a x ) ] for Models 1 , 2 , 3 , and 5 . This expression is plotted in Figure 2 . t e x simplifies in two limits: t e x = d if d ≪ τ m a x , and t e x = e − γ τ m a x if d ≫ τ m a x . For experiments, we expect the first limit to be relevant. These expressions, t e x = d , are given in Table 2. For Model 4, the stiffness deficit δ C is log-linear for all recovery times where τ m i n ≪ ξ ≪ τ m a x . There is no dependence on pump duration, so deviations from log-linearity do not appear until ξ ∼ τ m a x . Extrapolated time to full recovery is (19) t ex = e − γ τ m a x for Model 4 , independent of D . At ξ = t e x , δ C has the value − m E 1 ( e − γ ) = − m E 1 ( 0.5616 ) = − 0.49 m (Abramowitz and Stegun, 1972). A summary of the main results of Section 3, i.e., expressions for the laboratory accessible quantities slope m , floor B , and extrapolated time to full recovery t e x , is given in Table 2. 3.3 Remark: A successful model for conductivity relaxation in porous silicon Amir et al. (2009, 2011) proposed a model, similar to those above, for the log-linear aging of conductivity in porous silicon after conditioning by a large DC voltage for a "wait-time" D . This system is pumped quasi-statically, so there is no need for a smoothing approximation; the terms are already smooth. Their model neglected the possibility of saturation by means of a factor like 1 − N . It is thus the same as the smoothed Models 1 and 2 at low P . The resulting conductivities δ C ( t ) (described by Eqs. (11) and (16) at low P ) predict extrapolated times to recovery t e x = D that were found to agree with their measurements. They furthermore found it possible to deduce a value of τ m a x from their measurements at large D and ξ , and showed its temperature dependence as being in accord with a thermal activation hypothesis. 4 Numerical evaluations Solutions to the models may also be obtained by numerical integration, without smoothing. Such are presented below. Doing so allows investigation of the models' behaviors at fine time resolutions O ( 1 / ω ) that are masked by smoothing, and may allow discernment of any other limitations of the smoothing approximation. Model 1 was integrated numerically by Sens-Schönfelder et al. (2019). This section presents a complementary calculation using similar parameters. Detailed comparison is not possible, however, as their model also included contributions from assumed classical fast nonlinearities. Qualitative comparison does however help to confirm our numerics and provide confidence for the other models. Details of the numerical evaluations are given in Appendix B, with only the results presented here. 4.1 Pump phase Figure 3 portrays the SD response during the pump-phase ( t D ), Fig. 5 shows the 0.5 seconds after pump-off in Model 1 for the two pump strengths 0.01 and 5, each after pumping for duration D = 0.1 s. Log linear recovery is evident, with the high P case being log-linear over a greater range in ln ( t ) than the low P case as predicted by Eq. (16) (see also Eq. A8). Deviations from log-linear are also evident at times of order D for low P and ( 1 + P ) D for high P . Extrapolated time to full recovery t e x (open circles in Fig. (5)) should be 0.1 and 0.594 ( ln t e x = -2.3 and -0.52), according to Eq. (18). This is confirmed by examining the plots. The slope of recovery should be m = P / ( 1 + P ) . This is also confirmed (dashed red line in Fig. 5). The recovery period ( t > D ) for Models 3-5 is shown in Fig. 6 . For Model 3, the recovery is independent of the parameter ω τ m i n , as the simulations for low ω τ m i n (Fig. 6a, solid blue line) and high ω τ m i n (dotted yellow line) coincide (conditioning was for D = 0.1 s). Similar to Model 1 for low P , deviations from log-linear recovery occur at times of order D . As λ = E ω = 1 , the slope of recovery should be 1 / 2 (see Table 2); this is confirmed in Fig. 6a (dashed red line). Although the pump phase of Model 4 does not accumulate damage (Fig. 3e), the recovery is still logarithmic, as shown in Fig. 6b. The model exhibits no dependence on D . There are late-time roll-offs as δ C approaches zero, but these are governed by τ m a x rather than D . Extrapolated time to full recovery t e x is expected at e − γ τ m a x = 0.5616 τ m a x . For Model 5, Fig. 6c displays the log-linear recovery with the inset showing the familiar shape of damage accumulation and healing. Attention is drawn to its extrapolated time to full recovery ln ( t e x ) = 1.1 . This is slightly greater than the prediction ln ( ( 1 + z / 2 ) D ) = 0.96 (open circle in Fig. 6c, z = 50 ) based on the guess that P b can be replaced in Eq. 10 by 1 / 2 . It is much greater than the predictions of Models 1-3 for which ln ( t e x ) = ln ( D ) = − 2.3 . For all models, we find that the numerical simulation’s values for m and t e x correspond well to the smoothing-approximation's predictions (Table 2). 4.3 Summary of Sections 3 and 4 Within the smoothing approximation Models 1, 2a, and 2b differ only in their different predictions for how SD amplitudes scale with ω . The time-dependence of the smoothed δ C ( t ) are the same (Eq. 11 and 16), but there are differences on fine time scales of order 1 / ω (Fig. 4). All three models predict stiffness changes that scale with P / ( 1 + P ) , where P is linearly proportional to pump strain amplitude E , and varies with pump frequency like ω 1 , ω 0 , and ω 1 / 2 respectively. Model 3 predicts almost the same smoothed behaviors as do Models 1 and 2, albeit with some mostly negligible power law time dependences in addition to log time (Eq. 12 and A10). A chief difference is that the SD amplitude scales with E 2 ω 2 . Models 4 and 5 predict that SD amplitude is proportional to E and independent of pump frequency ω (Eq. 13 and 14). All models except 4 predict that stiffness falls like log(t) during the pump phase: δ C ( t ) ∼ c o n s t a n t − m ln ( t ) (Eqs. 11, 12, and 14). Model 4, on the other hand, predicts damage to be constant throughout the pump phase (Eq. 13). During recovery, all models predict that stiffness initially proceeds in a log-linear fashion like c o n s t a n t + m ln ( ξ ) , with ξ ≡ t − D but rolls off as δ C approaches full recovery δ C = 0 . The chief effect of duration D is in its effect on the extrapolated time to full recovery t e x (see Eq. 18 and Table 2). If D ≪ τ m a x , then t e x = ( 1 + P ) D for Models 1 and 2, t e x = D for Model 3, and t e x = ( 1 + z 2 ) D for Model 5. For Model 4, t e x is independent of D (Eq. 19). In the case of Model 5, t e x could potentially be much greater than D. 5 5 Models 1 and 2 also predict t e x greater than D : t e x = ( 1 + P ) D (see Table 2). But it cannot be substantially greater because large P would imply that SD (proportional to / ( 1 + P ) , Eq. 16) is nearly independent of E , contrary to laboratory observations (Shokouhi et al., 2017; TenCate et al., 2000; Yoritomo and Weaver, 2020a). The key predictions of the many models may be summarized: • Model 1: SD amplitude scales with P / ( 1 + P ) with P ∝ E ω . At low P ≪ 1 , stiffness deficit δ C scales linearly with pump strain amplitude E and pump strain frequency ω ; at large P ≫ 1 , the scaling with E and ω becomes sublinear or even constant. Prediction for extrapolated time to full recovery is t e x = ( 1 + P ) D . Loop senses agree with the lab observations (Lott et al., 2016; Renaud et al., 2013, 2012). • Model 2: As Model 1, but with P ∝ E and E ω 1 / 2 for 2a and 2b, respectively. Loop senses agree with (Lott et al., 2016; Renaud et al., 2013, 2012) for 2b but not 2a. • Model 3: SD amplitude scales like E 2 ω 2 . Extrapolated time to full recovery t e x = D . Loop senses agree with (Lott et al., 2016; Renaud et al., 2013, 2012). • Model 4: SD amplitude scales like E , independent of D and ω . Extrapolated time to full recovery t e x = e − γ τ m a x , also independent of D . Unlike the other models, it shows no increase of damage with time between pump-on and pump-off, regardless of pump duration. Loop senses disagree with (Lott et al., 2016; Renaud et al., 2013, 2012). • Model 5: Like Model 4, SD scales with E , but not ω . Extrapolated time to full recovery t e x ≈ ( 1 + z / 2 ) D , potentially much greater than D. Loop senses disagree with (Lott et al., 2016; Renaud et al., 2013, 2012). The next section presents laboratory SD measurements on three different structures and compares observed behaviors with the predictions of the above models. In particular, it seeks to confirm, or contradict, model predictions for the influence of E , D , and ω . 5 Experiments We measure slow dynamic damage and healing in three systems: 1) a bar of Berea sandstone with dimensions 25 × 36 × 305 mm and estimated bar wave speed c B = 2274 m/s, 2) a bar of concrete with dimensions 25.4 × 56 × 306 mm and 2.36-4.75 mm sized aggregate and estimated bar wave speed c B = 2890 m/s, and 3) a system consisting of a single 3 mm diameter aluminum bead confined between aluminum slabs of mass 2.75kg and dimensions 25 × 201 × 201 mm. Our focus is on quantifying dependence on the pump strain amplitude E , on the pump duration D , and on the pump strain's frequency ω . The laboratory set-up is summarized in Fig. 7 . For the Berea sandstone and concrete (Fig. 7b), the samples are epoxied to an aluminum disk that is bolted to the head of an electromagnetic shaker which drives the bar in longitudinal vibrations at frequencies from 100 to 4000 Hz. An accelerometer is screwed into a mounting that is glued to the top; it monitors longitudinal motions of the sample. (A laser vibrometer, not shown, is available to measure lateral motions.) For the single bead system (Fig. 7c), the bead is sandwiched between the two slabs, partially supporting the upper slab. The confining force on the bead is estimated to be 3.2N. A shaker driving a dead weight of 0.83kg sits on the lower slab and generates vibrations (300 – 1000 Hz) in the structure and oscillatory loads on the bead. An accelerometer is placed directly above the bead. For all three systems, the accelerometer signal is fed to an ancillary digital oscilloscope, permitting the operator to judge the quality of the pump vibrations and to infer the pump vibration strain amplitude (see Appendix C.1 for inference of strain in the Berea and concrete samples; see Yoritomo and Weaver (2020b) for the single bead). For the Berea and concrete samples, ultrasonic transducers are attached to the sides through removable phenyl salicylate glue (Fig. 7b); for the single bead, they are placed on the upper and lower slabs (Fig. 7c). The transducers send and receive a low amplitude high frequency continuous wave (CW) probe signal with frequency of order 300 kHz chosen as described in Appendix C.2. The digitizer/PC collects the CW reference signal g ( t ) that drives the source transducer and the transmitted signal h ( t ) from the receiver. It then extracts the phase difference Θ between them using a digital lock-in (DLI) calculation (Yoritomo, 2025; Yoritomo and Weaver, 2025). The phase is typically evaluated every 10 msec. The DLI phase difference Θ is our parameter for studying SD. Diminishment of sample modulus corresponds to slower ultrasonic wave speeds which in turn translates to greater phase delay. A typical plot of a DLI phase Θ over a 50 second test period was presented in Fig. (1) for the Berea sandstone sample. An initial ten second reference state was followed by D = 9.5 s of conditioning at a strain amplitude E = 1.64 μ strain and frequency f p u m p = 2300 Hz. The recovery was followed for an additional 30 seconds. Recording the ten second reference state permits subtraction of linear trends due to temperature drifts or residual SD recoveries from previous tests, though usually it is not necessary. Fig. 1b plots Θ vs. log ( t − t 0 ) during recovery, where t 0 is a reference time (Lee and Weaver, 2024) corresponding to the end of pumping and the beginning of recovery. Here it was taken at 15 msec after pump-off, related to the ring-down time of the pump vibrations. Fig. 8 displays typical DLI Phase Θ vs. time plots for the other systems. Details on how the changing DLI Phase is related to changes in coda-wave “stretch” δ c / c may be found in Appendix C2. For the studies described below, the interval between subsequent SD tests was at least 20 minutes. We emphasize here that measurements of the floor B are simply Θ at the time of pump-off and measurements of both the slope m and extrapolated time to fully recovery t e x are based on only the first few seconds of recovery data (for t e x a few seconds of pre-conditioning data are also needed to subtract off the reference state). N τ ( t ) (Eqs. 8-10) is predicted by all the models to have relaxed to near zero for all τ less than about 20 minutes (the time between tests). Because of this, remnant contributions to N τ ( t ) , if any, from previous tests are slowly varying enough not to contaminate the data used to construct t e x , m , or B . A virtue of focusing on these parameters—which only depend upon on a few seconds of data—is that we avoid issues related to the influence of initial conditions, previous conditioning, and slow temperature drifts, if any. The possibility that the Berea sandstone and concrete prisms warm during conditioning should be considered. If strong enough, such heating could distort the measured δ C . This hypothesis is discussed in Appendix C.3 and found unviable. 5.1 Pump Strain Amplitude Dependence Figure 9 shows the slope m of SD recoveries over a range of pump strain amplitudes E for Berea, concrete, and the single bead system. The pump duration was fixed at D = 10s for all measurements. The pump frequency was fixed for each sample as well. f p u m p was 2900 Hz for Berea and 2700 Hz for concrete. Two pump frequencies were chosen for the single bead system, 300 Hz and 1000 Hz. That SD recovery slope is linear with pump strain amplitude, at least above ∼1 μ strain, is evident for all three systems. For Berea and the single bead, the SD slope m varies linearly with pump amplitude for E > 1.2 μ strain and E > 0.6 μ strain, respectively, but cuts off at smaller strain. Fig. 9a is similar to figure 5 of TenCate et al. (2000), who also reported a threshold near 1 μ strain and linearity above that, but closer scrutiny will notice that they reported that their linear regime, when back-extrapolated to m = 0 , intersects at about 0.7 μ strain. Here that intersection occurs at negative strain. Yoritomo and Weaver (2020a) also observed a linear relationship between m and E for SD measurements of a glass bead pack. For concrete, there is no clear small strain threshold. Plots of the floor B vs. pump strain amplitude E have similar features for all three systems (Fig. 10 ). The pump strain amplitude dependence of B tracks that of m : B is linear with respect to E for all pump strains in concrete and for E > ∼1 μ strain for Berea and the single bead. There is an anomolous dip in B for the single bead around 0.6 μ strain for both pump frequencies. We conjecture that this is related to the small strain thresholds seen in Fig. 9c. 5.2 Pump duration dependence Figure 11 shows the influence of pump duration D on the extrapolated time to full recovery t e x in the three systems over three decades of duration (∼0.1s to ∼100s). The pump strain amplitude was held constant for each system, around 1 μ strain. t e x varies strongly with D , but the prediction of Models 1-3 that t e x is D itself is not supported (solid black lines in Fig. 11). Neither is the prediction of Model 4 that t e x = e − γ τ m a x , i.e., independent of D . It is strikingly clear that t e x ≫ D , especially for the concrete sample and single bead system. The data are consistent with Model 5 if a value of z ∼ 10 2 and τ m a x ∼ 10 3 s − 10 4 s is chosen. The sublinear behavior in Figs. 11a and 11c, which approximately follows t e x ∼ D , is possibly an indication of t e x approaching saturation at exp ( − γ ) τ m a x (see Fig. 2), especially if we allow for A ( τ ) to diminish gradually in the vicinity of some nominal τ m a x . The floor B and slope m also vary with D (Fig. 12 ), albeit much more slowly than t e x . That m rises with D is predicted by none of the models (Table 2), but the effect (approximately 50% increase) over three orders of magnitude in D is small. 5.3 Pump frequency dependence A study of SD dependence on pump frequency ω = 2 π f p u m p , i.e., a study of strain rate dependence, is more challenging than pump strain or pump duration. The pump strain amplitude E needs to be held fixed while the pump frequency is varied over a wide range. Yet the strain amplitude will in principle vary with frequency, e.g., due to resonances or a non-flat audio amplifier gain or shaker transfer function, and not always in a way that the accelerometer can track because the assumption (Appendix C.1) that the prism of Berea or concrete is in pure longitudinal motion may be violated if bending or torsion vibrations are excited. Other challenges follow from limitations due to finite strength of the shaker or the audio amplifier—not all strain levels are reachable at all frequencies. Much work in SD has taken advantage of resonances to enhance pump strain amplitude, but such is not possible when frequency dependence is to be explored. Figure 13 shows the observed SD recovery slopes from a series of tests over a range of pump frequencies (300 - 2700 Hz) for all three systems, while holding each data series’ inferred strain amplitude constant. For Berea and concrete, the pump strain amplitude was 1.47 and 1 μ strain, respectively; for the single bead system, two series were performed, one with E = 1 μ strain and the other with E = 2 μ strain. Pump duration was also fixed throughout ( D = 10 s ). The data are erratic with no dominating trend. We speculate that the irregularity is a function of sundry resonances, e.g bending or torsion. The presence of such is not detected by the accelerometer, leading to an erroneous estimate of total pump strain. This effect would not contaminate the above studies of E or D dependence, as there it simply rescales the presumed E . Except for the bump near 400 Hz, the concrete data would be clear: there is no dependence on frequency. The region near 400 Hz was examined more closely the next day, and appears to be an isolated resonance. This observation was supported by a separate examination of the lateral displacements using a Polytec laser vibrometer that confirmed that the lateral material velocity goes through a peak and a sharp phase shift (relative to forcing) at 400 Hz. While the structure is nominally symmetric, which should suppress bending waves, there may be symmetry breaking flaws or misalignments that couple the longitudinal motions and bending waves. The data of Fig. 13 suffer from some irregularities. It may be possible to reduce the irregularity by redesign of the experimental structure. Regardless, however, of the irregularities, one would be hard pressed to imagine that a hidden frequency proportionality has been obscured. There is no evidence for the predictions of Models 1, 2b and 3 for scaling with f p u m p , f p u m p , or f p u m p 2 respectively. Table 3 re-summarizes and compares the Models’ predictions for SD amplitude dependence and extrapolated time to full recovery t e x on the pump parameters E , D , and ω , as well as the sense [decreasing δ C at the crossing point ( ↘ ↙ ) vs. increasing δ C ( ↗ ↖ )] of DAET Loop plots (Fig. 4). The experimental results are summarized in the final column. Model 5 aligns best with the experiments. However, its prediction for the orientation of the Loop plots is opposite that of experiments. 6 Estimating the minimum relaxation time All five models predict that τ m i n is recoverable from measurements of m and B (recall that B = δ C ( t = D ) ). Setting t = D in Eq. (11), Models 1 and 2 predict that (20) ln ( τ m i n ) = γ + ln ( ( 1 + P ) D ) + B / m . If ω τ m i n ≫ 1 , Model 3 predicts an effectively identical expression (from Eq. 12): (21) ln ( τ m i n ) = γ + ln D + B / m . If ω τ m i n is small, extracting τ m i n from B / m at fixed ω using Eq. (12) would appear difficult. A series of measurements of B at different ω might point to τ m i n if ω τ m i n were of order unity or less. For Model 4, something similar is possible. From Eq. (13), (22) ln ( τ m i n ) = ln ( τ m a x ) + B / m . In the likely event that τ m a x is not known we could use measured log-linear fit values of δ C , evaluated at, say, ξ = 1 s e c , where δ C = m ( γ − ln ( τ m a x ) ) to construct an estimate of τ m a x . This permits us to write (23) ln ( τ m i n ) = γ − δ C f i t ( @ ξ = 1 s ) / m + B / m . Yoritomo and Weaver (2025) pointed out that the latter two terms describe the time where a back-extrapolated log-linear fit meets the floor. Thus, estimates of τ m i n can be obtained (Eq. 23) from the ratio of floor to slope m , and the ξ = 1 s value of the log-linear fit. For Model 5, Eq. (14) implies that (24) ln ( τ m i n ) = γ + ln ( ( 1 + z / 2 ) D ) + B / m . Like the other models it may permit estimates of τ m i n from measurements of the floor. The ratio B / m can be taken from the data displayed in Section 5. For Berea, the ratio is about -25 from the pump strain amplitude study and largely independent of E . From the pump duration study, the ratio is lower, about -35. Models 1-3 would estimate τ m i n to be between 0.25ns and 0.01ps (using the data from D = 10 s). Model 4 estimates τ m i n to be between 1.4ns and 0.6ps, if we conservatively estimate τ m a x as 100s. Model 5 estimates between 6ns and 0.3ps, for a choice of z = 50 . For concrete and the single bead, estimates of B / m from the pump strain amplitude study and pump duration study are more consistent, with the ratio being between -21 and -24 for concrete and between -4 and -9 for the single bead. For concrete, using B / m = − 22.5 , Models 1-3 estimate τ m i n as 3ns, Model 4 as 17ns, and Model 5 as 80ns (for z = 50 ). For the single bead, using B / m = − 6.5 , Models 1-3 estimate τ m i n as 0.03s, Model 4 as 0.15s, and Model 5 as 0.7s (for z = 50 ). Thus, estimates of τ m i n for the single bead system are many orders of magnitude larger than for the Berea sandstone or concrete samples. Although striking, this is consistent with previous attempts at inferring τ m i n from experimental data, where estimates of τ m i n for single bead systems were as much as 10 5 times larger than a sandstone sample (10ms vs. 50ns) (Yoritomo and Weaver, 2025). These disparate estimates of τ m i n based on B / m are so highly variable, and occasionally too small to be credited as even atomic scale (Debye times are surely of order picoseconds), that we are forced to question our interpretation of the floor B as governed by the same physics as governs the recoveries (slope m ). The next section offers some speculations. 7 Conclusions It has been found that none of the models successfully describe all the chief behaviors and parameter dependences seen in the lab. All models except 3 predict that SD amplitudes are proportional to pump strain amplitude—which is confirmed in the lab though with so far unexplained thresholds at about 1 μstrain, see Figs. 9 and 10. SD amplitude as independent of pump frequency is predicted by Models 2a, 4 and 5, and to a lesser extent 2b (and not predicted by Model 1 or 3). It is confirmed in the lab with some fluctuations (see Fig. 13). Extrapolated time to recovery t e x is predicted in all models (except 4) to linearly rise with pump duration, which is observed. However, the actual t e x are far greater than predicted by Models 1, 2 and 3. They are consistent with Model 5. Loop senses as reported by Renaud et al. (Renaud et al., 2013, 2012) and Lott et al. (Lott et al., 2016) match predictions from Models 1, 2b and 3, but not from 2a, 4 and 5. It may also be judged a failing that the models, when applied to observed SD slopes m and floors B , can imply scattered and sometimes strikingly tiny values for τ m i n . Linear scaling of modulus deficits with pump strain amplitude E (with a threshold near 1μ strain) has been noted elsewhere (TenCate et al., 2000; Yoritomo and Weaver, 2020a). That behavior is also supported here, except for the precise character of the threshold in Berea and the lack of a 1μ strain threshold in the concrete. 6 6 It has been suggested that measurements of strain amplitudes, inferred as they are from a dissipation-free model of pump wave propagation, may be in error, rendering the reports of linear scaling, or any statement about pump strain amplitude, unreliable. But as discussed in Appendix C.1, we do not believe that dissipation-free bar-wave modeling is problematic. The data here are clear that m and B are otherwise proportional to E . This is as predicted by all the models, except Model 3. 7 7 Zeman et al. (2025) accommodate their prediction that m and B are proportional to E 2 , which disagrees with experiments, by taking their parameter λ to scale inversely with E (so that m ∼ λ E 2 = E ) . The large laboratory measured extrapolated times to recovery t e x are striking, Fig. 11. Models 1-3 all predict recoveries proceeding like δ C = m [ ln ( ξ ) − ln ( ξ + d ) ] , where d is the effective pump duration ( 1 + P ) D and ξ is time since pump-off. This implies t e x = d ≈ D (linear scaling with E implies P ≪ 1 ). Amir et al. (2009, 2011) predicted the same recovery in their study of conductivity aging in porous silicon. But their measurements, unlike ours, agreed with the prediction. The present tests all find t e x to be many times greater than D (consistent, one might add, with seismic observations of pump durations of ∼minutes followed by months of recovery). The large difference t e x ≠ D is a significant quantitative discrepancy. Models 4 and 5, however, in which damage is posited to accumulate more quickly than healing appear to have explained it (Table 2). Heating during the pump phase could also explain an apparent t e x > D , but arguments (Appendix C.3) find the hypothesis unviable. The laboratory data indicate that SD recovery slope m and floor B are independent of pump frequency. The Berea and concrete data, though irregular, are unequivocal in observing no sign of linear, let alone quadratic, scaling with frequency. Scaling with pump frequency ω as in Model 1 would be hard to reconcile with the well-known presence of SD over a wide range of frequencies including quasi-static, i.e., ω ∼ 0 , pumping, e.g., (Yoritomo and Weaver, 2020a, 2020b). It is a virtue of Model 3 that it posits pumping by a combination of strain and strain rate. But in the parameter range studied here, where ξ ω is large, Model 3 predicts SD recovery slope to scale simply with ω 2 (Eq. 16 and Table 2) and recovery to have a time dependence like that of the other models, differing only by negligible power law corrections. Model 3 also differs from the other models in predicting a depression in the pump phase floor if ω τ m i n 0 after the end of pumping, the modulus deficit is (A5) δ C = − A ¯ P 1 + P [ I ( ξ ) − I ( ( 1 + P ) D + ξ ) ] . Inference of dynamic strain from measured acceleration The strain at the midpoint of the bar ϵ ( L / 2 ) as inferred from accelerations at the top of the bar a t o p is given by (C1) ϵ ( L 2 ) = a t o p ( L 2 c 2 ) ( sin ( k L 2 ) k L / 2 + 2 μ M cos ( k L 2 ) ) , where M is the bar mass, L is its length, and μ is the accelerometer's mass. This relation is derived from the vertical displacement's known dependence on the longitudinal coordinate x : (C2) u ( x ) = U cos ( k x ) + V sin ( k x ) , where k is the pump wavenumber ω / c b a r ( ω = 2 π f p u m p ), x is distance below the top and u ( x ) is material displacement in the downward direction. A factor e i ω t has been suppressed. The top's acceleration (in the downward direction) is (C3) a t o p = − U ω 2 The strain within the bar is (C4) ϵ ( x ) = d u d x = − U k sin ( k x ) + V k cos ( k x ) Force balance at the top requires (where Y is Young's modulus and A is the bar's cross-sectional area) (C5) μ a t o p = Y A ϵ ( x = 0 ) = Y A k V . A bit of algebra, and recalling that Y = ρ c b a r 2 and M = ρ L A , where ρ is mass density, then reveals the stated relation (Eq. C1). Similar calculations permit us to predict the acceleration at the base in terms of the acceleration of the top (C6) a b a s e = a t o p ( cos ( k L ) − μ M k L sin ( k L ) ) . This latter relation was confirmed by measurements of both accelerations. The good correspondence gives us confidence that strain is being properly inferred. In particular, our neglect of attenuation is not significant. Inference of strain from top acceleration relies upon an assumption that the sample is in longitudinal ("bar-wave") motion with no significant attenuation. If longitudinal vibrations are in fact attenuated (and SD is known to enhance attenuation) inference of strain may be in error. Estimates of the effect do not, however, support a hypothesis that inferences could be seriously in error due to neglect of enhanced attenuation. Johnson and Sutin (2005) measured quality factor changes in various materials and found them to be proportional to wave-speed changes by δ ( 1 / Q ) = − γ S D δ c / c with γ S D having values of 0.2 to 0.4. This tells us that while the fractional change in quality factor δ Q / Q can be large if Q is initially large (because δ Q / Q = γ S D Q δ c / c ), the enhancement of loss tangent δ ( 1 / Q ) to which attenuation is proportional remains small when δ c / c is small. δ c / c is typically of order of order 10 − 3 or less. C.2 Recovering fractional wavespeed changes from DLI phase What do changes in the DLI measured transfer function h ( ω ) ≡ exp [ a ( ω ) + i Θ ( ω ) ] , and in particular changes in Θ , imply for stretch? The literature (Brenguier et al., 2008; Johnson et al., 1996; Kober et al., 2025; Renaud et al., 2013; TenCate et al., 2000; Yoritomo and Weaver, 2020a) tends to report stretches s = − δ c / c (fractional wavespeed changes) or equivalently − δ f / f (fractional change in a resonant frequency) or δ C / C (fractional change in a modulus). This raises the question as to how Θ translates to stretches? Define ψ ˜ ( ω ) as the complex Fourier transform of the pulse-receive waveform ψ ( t ) : (C7) ψ ˜ ( ω ) = ∫ 0 ∞ ψ ( t ) e − i ω t d t = e α ( ω ) + i ϕ ( ω ) The phase of the Fourier transform ϕ = I m [ ln ( ψ ˜ ) ] = arg ( ψ ˜ ) will be negative. Any delays in ψ ( t ) will be associated with ϕ being more negative. For real ω , we can obtain ψ ˜ at any frequency f p r o b e by comparing steady state harmonic CW inputs and outputs, e.g by DLI. We could also construct ψ ˜ by numerical Fourier transform Eq. (C7) of the time-domain waveform (and do so for complex ω as well if we want, simply by inserting a factor exp ( − σ t ) before integrating). Equivalence of the two constructions depends upon the assumed linearity of the probe wave and the delta-function character of the pulser output. We are interested in a perturbed pulse/receive waveform of the form ψ ′ ( t ) = ψ ( t / ( 1 + s ) ) e − σ t , where s is the stretch (positive values indicate a dilation, i.e., a delay) = − δ c / c , and σ is the increased absorption. Johnson and Sutin (2005) observed that σ changes in proportion to stretch; σ = ( γ / 2 ) ω s , where γ depends on the material and on whether the system is in the pump phase or in recovery. Typically, γ ∼ 0.3 . We inquire regarding the corresponding change in the imaginary part of the log of ψ ˜ ( ω ) at some fixed probe frequency ω : (C8) a ( ω ) + i Θ ( ω ) = ln ( ψ ˜ ′ ( ω ) ) − ln ( ψ ˜ ( ω ) ) Θ is the DLI phase change. We then note (C9) ψ ˜ ' ( ω ) = ∫ ∞ 0 ψ ( t 1 + s ) e − σ t e − i ω t dt ≈ ( 1 + s ) ∫ ∞ 0 ψ ( ζ ) e − i ω ζ ( 1 + s ) − σ ζ d ζ = ( 1 + s ) ψ ˜ ( ω + s ω − i σ ) and ψ ˜ ( ω + s ω − i σ ) = exp [ α ( ω ′ ) + i ϕ ( ω ′ ) ] , where ω ′ = ω + s ω − i s ω γ 2 . The change in the log of the perturbed transfer function is (using ln ( 1 + s ) ≈ s ) (C10) a + i Θ = s + α ( ω ′ ) + i ϕ ( ω ′ ) − α ( ω ) − i ϕ ( ω ) . DLI phase Θ is then found to be related to stretch s by (C11) Θ = I m [ s + α ( ω ′ ) + i ϕ ( ω ′ ) − α ( ω ) − i ϕ ( ω ) ] . If we may neglect γ , this simplifies: (C12) Θ = ϕ ( ω + s ω ) − ϕ ( ω ) ≈ s ω ∂ ϕ ∂ ω Otherwise, we'd write: (C13) Θ = I m [ α ( ω ′ ) ] + R e [ ϕ ( ω ′ ) − ϕ ( ω ) ] ≈ s ω [ ∂ ϕ ∂ ω − ( γ 2 ) ∂ α ∂ ω ] Similar expressions could be derived for DLI amplitudes a . The above formula suggests how to choose an optimal probe frequency. One might in particular wish to choose a probe frequency where ϕ ( ω ) is locally linear in ω , such that Θ is linear in s over the anticipated range of values of s or Θ , so that the simple expression Θ ≈ s ω ∂ ω ϕ ( ω ) is adequate. More generally, the expression Θ = ϕ ( ω + s ω ) − ϕ ( ω ) may be inverted to effect nonlinear calibrations s ( Θ ) regardless of local linearity of ϕ ( ω ) . Figure C1 illustrates the relation Θ ( s ) and the process of choosing a probe frequency so that the relation is smooth and linear. In practice we start our tests by collecting a pulse receive waveform ψ ( t ) , and then selecting a probe frequency for which arg ( ψ ˜ ) is locally linear in frequency, Eq. C12, making sure that linearity is preserved over the expected range of Θ or stretch. Typical sensitivities are of order hundreds of radians per stretch. Fig. C1 Illustration of the relationship between DLI phase Θ and stretch. Panel (a) presents a typical time-domain waveform ψ ( t ) between the two ultrasound transducers. (b) shows the absolute value of its Fourier transform | ψ ˜ ( ω ) | . Panel (c) presents the phase ϕ ( ω ) of ψ ˜ ( ω ) . Panel (d) plots Θ = ϕ ( ω + s ω ) − ϕ ( ω ) (Eq C12) for a chosen probe frequency f p r o b e = 395 kHz ( ω = 790000 π ). The stated slope of -582 describes the region of smaller stretch values s < 0.003 where the relation is highly linear. In practice, probe frequency is chosen by examining plots like (d) until good linearity is achieved. Fig C1 dummy alt text C.3 Estimates of heat generation in Berea sandstone and concrete prisms The possibility that our prisms warm during conditioning should be considered. If great enough, such could distort the measured δ C . Examination of recovery curves like those of Figs. 1 and 8a shows that heating by an amount sufficient to call for a correction that would raise theta after pump-off by 0.02 and 0.04 radians respectively would thereby shorten the inferred t e x to a value near D . This would have consequences for distinguishing amongst the models. DLI Phase Θ values of 0.02 and 0.04 radians can be converted to temperature rises if we take TenCate et al.'s (2003) report that δ c / c in sandstone is of order 2 × 10 − 4 per degree, and if we take a typical conversion between δ c / c and Θ of 400 radians per stretch. The result is about 0.25 to 0.50 degree. Given a sandstone heat capacity of 880 J/kg degree, one concludes that such a temperature rise requires about 220 to 440 Joules of heat to be deposited in the prisms, over about 10 seconds. Zeman et al. (2025) suggest that the prisms could gain heat by internal friction. This is not viable. The vibration energy in the prism is Y E 2 * V o l u m e (where Y is Young’s modulus). This converts to heat at a rate π f / Q , and does so over the conditioning time D . Taking crude values D = 10 , Q = 100 , f = 2400 / s, E = 1 μ strain and c b a r = 3000 m/s, we determine that the net heat deposition is 2 × 10 − 7 Joules per kg. This is negligible, and remains so even if one adjusts the guessed parameters. They also suggest that heat could be generated in their PZT actuator disk and conducted to the probe region at the midpoint of the prism. We do not know the heat generation rate in the actuator, but we can estimate how well it would diffuse axially. Given heat diffusivity of 1.1 m m 2 / s , one deduces a penetration distance of [ 4 * Diffusivity * D ] 1 / 2 = 6.6 mm, far shorter than the 150mm distance from the shaker to the probe region. (In the system studied by Zeman et al., diffusion over their conditioning time of 180 sec is 28 mm, also small compared to their 75 mm distance to the probe region.) This mechanism is not viable either; axial heat diffusion is far too slow. We may also consider a conjecture that heat is generated in the shaker and convected through the air (heat rises) to the probe region where it then must diffuse laterally to the interior. The heat generation rate is at least the Joule heating due to the measured 0.6 Ohms of electrical resistance times the square of typical currents (∼1 Ampere in the tests conducted at variable D ), i.e., 0.6 Watts. In ten seconds, this deposits 6 Joules, far less than the needed 220 to 440 Joules. Heat generated by mechanical resistance within the shaker may also be estimated, by evaluating ∼ ( 1 kg rigid body mass ) * ( rms velocity ) 2 * ( 10 sec 10 msec ) ∼ 0.016 J . (The 10 msec value comes from the known ringdown rate after pump-off; rms velocity ∼ acceleration ω = 60 m e t e r / s e c 2 15000 r a d / s e c ). The conjecture further requires that the heat diffuse laterally through the prism from the air. This occurs at an eigenrate no faster than that given by ( Diffusivity ) * π 2 * ( 1 L x 2 + 1 L y 2 ) = 0.025 per second, but is slowed further by imperfect conduction from air to the surface of the stone. The net rate was measured by Zeman et al. (2025) and found to be about 0.001 / s e c . In conclusion, this process is too weak (at least for our shaker) and too slow to have significantly distorted our measurements. References Abramowitz and Stegun, 1972 M. Abramowitz I. title: Nonreciprocal transition waves in active lattices
journal: Journal of the Mechanics and Physics of Solids
abstract: Active systems, such as contraction of muscle fibers and action potential propagation in neural networks, exhibit efficient directional transport of information and energy. Bringing these biological principles into engineered systems can overcome the limitations of passive metamaterials in tunability after fabrication, spontaneous reset, and the realization of dynamical nonreciprocity. However, a unified and transparent analytical framework for reversible nonreciprocal wave propagation governed by coupled fast-slow dynamics remains absent. We develop a mechanical lattice composed of active bistable elements incorporating a displacement-driven slow recovery variable to emulate biologically inspired recovery processes and capture the evolution of active stiffness. Discrete and continuum models are derived to describe the lattice dynamics. Building on continuum descriptions, we employ singular perturbation and matched asymptotic expansions, complemented by machine learning, to enable analytical characterization. Then we define a metric of nonreciprocal energy transport and map the key parameters to quantify how system parameters jointly control wave speed and propagation direction. Nonlinear results reveal that forward and backward transition waves propagate at markedly different steady speeds, demonstrating pronounced nonreciprocity. The key mechanism arises from the coupling between fast excitation and slow recovery, which produces a refractory effect that resets the energy landscape and therefore breaks time reversal symmetry in a geometrically symmetric structure. During the evolution of active stiffness, an intermediate phase emerges and co-propagates with the mechanical wave and selectively suppresses returning waves under appropriate timing conditions. Consequently, reversible and tunable nonreciprocal transition waves are achieved. We obtain bidirectional wave solutions, along with analytical expressions for wave speed, width and energy flux as well as their conditions of validity. Theoretical predictions agree closely with numerical simulations and demonstrate that the nonreciprocity depends on the recovery rate in a nonlinear manner. This study provides a general theoretical framework and practical design guidelines for bioinspired intelligent metamaterials that enable reversible directional energy routing, signal isolation, and mechanical logic, and it also informs the design of field controlled phase change materials and multiphysics devices.

S0022-5096(26)00197-3 10.1016/j.jmps.2026.106697 Elsevier Ltd Fig. 1 The active self-resetting bistable lattice with tunable energy landscape capable of propagating transition waves, inspired by sarcomere dynamics. (a) Schematic of a muscle fiber composed of myofibrils. (b) Myofibrillar structure showing periodic arrangement of actin and myosin filaments regulated by biological energy fields, maintaining stability before and after contraction. (c) Force-deformation relationship in a sarcomere, where active and passive forces establish bistable states under biological energy field influence. (d) Bistable lattice consisting of active units connected by elastic elements (stiffness k), with stable state separation 2L. The force-displacement response combines active potential φ a (field-driven) and passive potential φ p (intrinsic stiffness). (e)-(g) Dependence of potential energy U i and force fi on displacement ui for si (e) varied from 5e-4 to 5e-3 g · m − 2 · s − 2 , A 0 (f) varied from 5e-4 to 1e-2 g · m − 2 · s − 2 , and A 1 (g) varied from 5e-4 to 5e-3 g · m − 2 · s − 2 , where the energy landscape of the active bistable lattice is reset as si increases. Active stiffness modulation transforms the energy landscape, creating asymmetric potential distributions that induce nonreciprocity. (h)-(j) Transition wave propagation for ξ = 0 (h), ξ = 0.03 (i), and ξ = 0.06 s − 1 (j). The system sustains transition waves without energy injection at ξ = 0 s − 1 , while active control enables transition wave propagation of ui and si at higher ξ values. Parameters: m = 1 g , k = 1 g · s − 2 , η = 1 g · s − 1 , N = 101 , a = 0.002 m , B n = 2 g · m − 2 · s − 2 ( n = 0 , 1 ) , L = 1 m , δ = 2 . (a)-(c) Spatiotemporal evolution of ui (t) and si (t) for inactive (a), reciprocal (b), and nonreciprocal (c) systems. Control of si (t) enables unidirectional waves (a), constant-speed bidirectional waves (b), and asymmetric bidirectional waves (c) without external resetting forces. (d)-(f) Spatiotemporal dynamics at ξ = 0.05 s − 1 (d) with snapshots at t = 250 s (e) and t = 750 s (f), revealing distinct waveforms, speeds (forward 3.302e-4 m/s, backward 1.005e-4 m/s), and widths for forward versus backward propagation. (g) Wave speed versus ξ showing nonlinear decrease for both directions. (h) and (i) Mechanical wave width (ui ) (h) and active wave width (si ) (i) versus ξ, exhibiting nonlinear variation with opposite trends for forward and backward waves. Fig. 2 dummy alt text Fig. 3 Numerical results of the continuum model: analysis of the relative error field for the two-variable coupled dimensionless continuum model, variations of waveform, wave speeds and wave width with ζ, B ¯ 0 and B ¯ 1 , and propagation of multiple controllable transition waves. The continuous model shows reasonable agreement with u ¯ i ( t ) errors remaining small, while s ¯ i ( t ) errors reflect increased sensitivity to backward wave initiation timing at larger ζ. (d)-(f) Waveform and wave speed analysis of forward and backward transition waves at varying ζ = 0.1 ∼ 0.315 (d), B ¯ 0 = 0.8 ∼ 1.2 (e), and B ¯ 1 = 0.8 ∼ 1.2 (f). (g)-(i) Mechanical and active wave widths versus ζ (g), B ¯ 0 (h), and B ¯ 1 (i) for both models when ζ = 0.35 . Nonlinear variations demonstrate tunable nonreciprocity. (j) Controllable return position of backward transition waves, showing programmable stopping of forward waves and on-demand initiation of backward waves. (k) and (l) Multiple realizations of bidirectional transition waves, demonstrating two (k) or more (l) cycles with selectable return positions and stable repeated propagation. (a)Asymptotic expansion solution of the forward mechanical wave u ¯ f at t ¯ = 2500 . (b)-(c) Standalone asymptotic expansion solution of the forward active wave s ¯ f and asymptotic expansion solutions (b) coupled with Random Forest, Neural Networks, Support Vector Regression, and Polynomial Regression, along with their mean squared errors (c). (d) Perturbation expansion solution of the backward mechanical wave u ¯ b at t ¯ = 4800 . (e)-(f) Standalone perturbation solution of the backward active wave s ¯ b and perturbation solutions (e) coupled with Random Forest, Neural Networks, Support Vector Regression, and Polynomial Regression, along with their mean squared errors (f). Random forest best captures active wave details with minimum error (c, f) when combined with asymptotic solutions. (g)-(i) Spatiotemporal evolution of forward and backward waves coupled with random forest for δ = 2 (g), δ = 4 (h), and δ = 6 (i). (j)-(l) Spatiotemporal evolution for ζ = 0.005 (j), ζ = 0.05 (k), and ζ = 0.1 (l). The hybrid method accurately reproduces continuous model results and captures intermediate active stiffness states. (a)-(c) Forward dimensionless wave speed (a), backward dimensionless wave speed (b) and the nonreciprocity (c) in the active bistable lattice over the parameter domain of ζ and δ. (d)-(f) Forward wave width (d), backward wave width (e) and the width nonreciprocity (f) in the active bistable lattice over the parameter domain of ζ and δ. The perturbation solution was found to accurately describe both the forward and backward wave speeds and widths, as well as the nonreciprocity of the system with respect to wave speed and width. The nonreciprocity N rel and the width nonreciprocity N width of the active bistable lattice exhibit a complex nonlinear distribution over the parameter domain (ζ, δ). The region of strongest nonreciprocity is concentrated near δ = 1.5 (excluding δ = 1.5 itself), and the wave speed is much less sensitive to ζ than to δ, while the width shows the opposite behavior. (g)-(j) Variation of nonreciprocal energy transmission with δ and with ( B ¯ 0 + B ¯ 1 ) / A ¯ 1 (g), ( B ¯ 0 − B ¯ 1 ) / A ¯ 1 (h), A ¯ 0 / A ¯ 1 (i) and B ¯ 0 / B ¯ 1 (j), respectively. The nonreciprocity of energy transmission in active bistable lattices exhibits a localized distribution within the parameter domain and is concentrated at the boundary regions of the parameter space. Active systems, such as contraction of muscle fibers and action potential propagation in neural networks, exhibit efficient directional transport of information and energy. Bringing these biological principles into engineered systems can overcome the limitations of passive metamaterials in tunability after fabrication, spontaneous reset, and the realization of dynamical nonreciprocity. However, a unified and transparent analytical framework for reversible nonreciprocal wave propagation governed by coupled fast-slow dynamics remains absent. We develop a mechanical lattice composed of active bistable elements incorporating a displacement-driven slow recovery variable to emulate biologically inspired recovery processes and capture the evolution of active stiffness. Discrete and continuum models are derived to describe the lattice dynamics. Building on continuum descriptions, we employ singular perturbation and matched asymptotic expansions, complemented by machine learning, to enable analytical characterization. Then we define a metric of nonreciprocal energy transport and map the key parameters to quantify how system parameters jointly control wave speed and propagation direction. Nonlinear results reveal that forward and backward transition waves propagate at markedly different steady speeds, demonstrating pronounced nonreciprocity. The key mechanism arises from the coupling between fast excitation and slow recovery, which produces a refractory effect that resets the energy landscape and therefore breaks time reversal symmetry in a geometrically symmetric structure. During the evolution of active stiffness, an intermediate phase emerges and co-propagates with the mechanical wave and selectively suppresses returning waves under appropriate timing conditions. Consequently, reversible and tunable nonreciprocal transition waves are achieved. We obtain bidirectional wave solutions, along with analytical expressions for wave speed, width and energy flux as well as their conditions of validity. Theoretical predictions agree closely with numerical simulations and demonstrate that the nonreciprocity depends on the recovery rate in a nonlinear manner. This study provides a general theoretical framework and practical design guidelines for bioinspired intelligent metamaterials that enable reversible directional energy routing, signal isolation, and mechanical logic, and it also informs the design of field controlled phase change materials and multiphysics devices. Keywords Nonreciprocal transition waves Mechanical metamaterials Self-resetting active bistable lattices Fast-slow dynamics Singular perturbation analysis Nonreciprocal energy transmission Data availability Data will be made available on request. Δ E ¯ Nonreciprocal energy-transport difference (nondimensional). 1 Introduction Understanding how ordered structures and spatiotemporal patterns emerge in complex systems remains a central scientific challenge (Greilich et al., 2025; Tian and Gong, 2025; Zhang et al., 2024; Zhou and Wang, 2024). In contrast to linear media that obey the principle of superposition, many systems can host energy in highly localized, particle like packets that propagate without change of shape. These self-sustained wave packets are termed solitary waves, and those with elastic scattering are classified as solitons (Gao et al., 2024; Silling, 2016; Yasuda et al., 2019; Zhang and Rudykh, 2024; Ziv and Shmuel, 2020). Among the many families of solitons, transition waves, i.e., elastic topological solitons, represent a canonical class distinguished by their role as smooth transition structures between two spatial regions associated with inequivalent degenerate ground states. Since their discovery, solitons have attracted sustained attention across physics, mathematics, and mechanics. From the KdV equation describing shallow water waves to constitutive models for biological tissues, soliton theory offers a unified mathematical framework spanning scales from molecular motion to macroscopic biomechanics (Tang et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2023; Zhou and Wang, 2023). The hallmarks of solitons are their resilience to dispersion during propagation and their persistence of shape after collisions. Transition waves (elastic topological solitons) interpolate continuously between different states, creating a robust interface whose existence and stability stem from the topology of the underlying potential landscape. This inherent topological protection makes them promising carriers of information and energy in mechanical settings (Zhang et al., 2019; Zhou and Wang, 2023). Bistability, characterized by two stable equilibria separated by an energy barrier, provides a broad physical and mechanical basis for the emergence of transition waves. Transition waves in bistable media therefore propagate as traveling elastic topological solitons with distinctive dynamics. These concepts have been applied to model and interpret a range of biophysical processes (see Fig. 1 (a)-(c)), including muscle fiber contraction (Caruel et al., 2015; Caruel and Truskinovsky, 2017; Givli and Bhattacharya, 2009), cytoskeletal remodeling (Bernitt et al., 2017; Pierre, 2020), and nerve impulse propagation. Rapid advances in mechanical metamaterials have created new opportunities for studying transition waves through architected control of macroscopic response. Guided by geometric nonlinearity, researchers have realized bistable building blocks based on buckled beams, shear units, and origami inspired modules. When arranged into one-, two- or three-dimensional lattices, these assemblies display strong nonlinear behavior and can store and release substantial elastic energy (Choi et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025a; Liu et al., 2019; 2023; Roller et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2024a; Xiu et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2022). In passive bistable media, the propagation of transition waves has been investigated in depth. A canonical model treats the architecture as rigid masses coupled by nonlinear springs whose force-displacement relation is nonmonotonic. When the system resides in a high energy metastable configuration, a local disturbance can trigger the first unit to switch to the low energy state. The released potential energy is transmitted to neighboring units through coupling, initiating a self-sustained front. This front propagates at an approximately constant speed and progressively converts the structure to the low energy configuration (Deng et al., 2020; 2025; Jin et al., 2020; Nadkarni et al., 2016a; Pal and Sitti, 2023; Raney et al., 2016; Zareei et al., 2020). Theoretical descriptions introduce a traveling wave ansatz that reduces governing partial differential or discrete difference equations to ordinary differential equations. This reduction enables analytical or semi-analytical expressions for the wave speed and profile. (Comte et al., 1999; Deng et al., 2020; Nadkarni et al., 2014; Remoissenet and Peyrard, 1984; Slepyan et al., 2005; Vasios et al., 2021). Experiments with carefully designed specimens have provided strong validation of these predictions. Such studies deepen the understanding of nonlinear lattice dynamics and inform the development of functional materials and devices, including high efficiency impact energy absorbers, passive mechanical logic elements, and acoustic diodes. Despite substantial progress, passive bistable media exhibit intrinsic limitations. The chief constraint is the unidirectional irreversibility and narrow functionality of wave propagation. Because energy release follows the second law of thermodynamics, once a wave has traversed the structure, the system relaxes to a low energy ground state and loses functional activity. Restoring functionality typically requires external mechanical resetting of the entire structure or selected regions. This requirement is impractical for applications that demand rapid and repeated responses (Nadkarni et al., 2014). A further limitation is that key response characteristics, such as wave speed and energy dissipation rate, are fixed by material and geometric parameters after fabrication. These properties cannot be adjusted during operation (Raney et al., 2016). These limitations motivate the exploration of approaches that introduce active control, so that the energy can be injected in a programmable and timely manner, thereby endowing the system with dynamically reconfigurable behavior. Active matter consists of dynamic assemblies of units that continuously consume energy and sustain nonequilibrium states, often converting environmental energy into mechanical motion or other forms of ordered behavior (Binysh et al., 2022; Da Rocha et al., 2022; Kulkarni, 2023; Wang et al., 2024c). In the field of bio-inspired engineering, researchers have introduced active mechanisms into bistable metamaterials, establishing the field of active bistable systems (Wu et al., 2024; Zhang and Rudykh, 2024; Zhou et al., 2022). A defining feature is the ability to modulate the energy wells through external stimuli. Examples include molecular motors powered by ATP hydrolysis, light-induced protein conformational changes, or chemical energy driving shape memory materials. Real time tunability overcomes the constraints of passive platforms and enables reversible propagation of phase transition waves. By regulating energy input with precision, the structure can switch between two stable states in a controlled manner, imparting bioinspired adaptability and intelligent responses (Roh et al., 2025; Song et al., 2025; Worrell et al., 2018). This strategy opens avenues for next generation smart materials with potential in micro and nanorobotics, adaptive structures, and biomedical engineering. The sarcomere in muscle is a typical example of the active bistable structure (see Fig. 1(a)-(c)) (Caruel et al., 2015). At the onset of sarcomere contraction, active elements consume energy to generate the primary driving force. As the deformation increases, the passive force rises while the active force decreases. This interplay enables a bistable mechanism governed by the combined action of active and passive forces throughout the process. Such systems are widespread in nature, from molecular motors and ciliary arrays to collective cell migration, and they exhibit distinctive nonequilibrium dynamics (Gerwien et al., 2019). The introduction of activity also introduces significant dynamical complexity. Accurate modeling requires multivariable coupling that augments mechanical displacement with additional variables representing active processes, such as active stress or active stiffness (Li et al., 2025b; Sun et al., 2025; Xing et al., 2024; Zhang and Rudykh, 2025). The resulting governing equations are strongly nonlinear. A prominent characteristic is the separation of time scales. Because mechanical displacement and unit flipping occur on a fast scale that can reach the millisecond range, whereas the evolution of active variables and the remodeling of the energy landscape proceed on a slow scale determined by the rate of the energy injection (Angeli et al., 2004; Göktepe et al., 2014). This coupling of fast and slow dynamics creates deep analogies with biological excitable media. The FitzHugh-Nagumo model, which combines a fast membrane potential with a slow gating variable to capture action potential generation and the refractory period, serves as a useful conceptual template. An active bistable lattice can be viewed as a mechanical counterpart of this excitable recoverable dynamics (FitzHugh, 1955; 1961). The complex behavior arises from strong nonlinear interactions between fast and slow variables, yielding rich dynamical regimes and posing significant challenges for theoretical analysis. This analogy offers a fresh perspective on the mechanics of living systems and guides the design of new classes of intelligent materials. The intrinsic coupling introduced by a slow recovery variable furnishes a new physical paradigm for realizing nonreciprocal wave propagation. Nonreciprocity denotes wave behavior that differs for forward and backward transmission and it underpins advanced platforms for multi-directional asymmetric information flow and directional energy transport (Shaat and Park, 2023; Tang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025). Conventional passive metamaterials primarily exploit carefully designed structural asymmetry to break spatial inversion symmetry, thereby realizing a static, geometry-dependent form of nonreciprocity. In contrast, active metamaterials enable a dynamic form of nonreciprocity that arises from the breaking of time-reversal symmetry. This behavior is fundamentally rooted in nonlinear interactions between fast and slow dynamical processes (Li et al., 2018; 2020; Librandi et al., 2021; Nassar et al., 2020). When a forward traveling front sweeps the medium, it triggers a rapid mechanical response and simultaneously drives an associated slow variable away from equilibrium. As this slow variable relaxes over a finite time, a nonequilibrium region forms in the wake that retains a memory of recent activity. A backward-propagating wave initiated during this relaxation stage encounters a medium reshaped by the evolving energy landscape rather than its initial state. This history dependent interaction leads to a pronounced difference between backward and forward propagation speeds, even when the geometric layout is perfectly symmetric (Tang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024d). This intrinsic arrow of time, established by the recovery dynamics of the slow variable, is conceptually analogous to the directional control of contraction-relaxation signal transmission in sarcomeres. In muscle fiber contraction, a refractory period governed by ion-channel gating ensures robust unidirectional propagation of contraction and relaxation signals along the muscle fibers, respectively (Beck et al., 2008; Rubin and Wechselberger, 2008). The proposed dynamic mechanism provides a mechanical realization of this biological principle. Most importantly, it frees nonreciprocal function from dependence on complex static geometry and enables real time in situ programmability. Control over the kinetics of the slow recovery variable allows continuous tuning of both the magnitude and the direction of nonreciprocity, opening a path to reconfigurable and environment-responsive intelligent nonreciprocal devices. Existing theories in mechanics and materials struggle to unify activity, reversibility, nonreciprocity, and coupling between fast and slow dynamics. For an active bistable lattice described by a two-variable coupled framework, the propagation of reversible nonreciprocal transition waves still lacks analytical clarity. Here, we introduce a slow recovery variable, governed by a recovery law with a time constant that describes the recovery process, where the displacement determines the target stiffness. This law is isomorphic to muscle activation dynamics in biomechanics and encodes a memory wake and a transiently inexcitable zone that modulate macroscopic transport direction. Building on this framework, we construct a lattice of masses connected by linear springs, with each mass anchored through passive and active bistable elements in parallel. By employing singular perturbation analysis to address the multiscale nonlinearity induced by fast-slow coupling, we derive bidirectional wave solutions, obtain analytical expressions for wave speed, width, and their domains of validity, and ultimately couple these solutions with a machine learning model. The results quantify how speed depends on passive and active stiffness parameters as well as on dimensionless recovery rate of active stiffness. A quantitative metric for nonreciprocal energy transport is established, demonstrating that even in geometrically symmetric media, reversible and tunable directional energy routing and signal isolation can be achieved by adjusting the timescale and coupling strength of the slow recovery variable. The theory provides explicit design principles and parameter maps for bioinspired active metamaterials. The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the mathematical model of the active bistable lattice, specifies the form of its energy and the self-resetting mechanism. Section 3 numerically solves the discrete model to visualize reversible and nonreciprocal transition waves, revealing an intermediate phase during the evolution of active stiffness. Section 4 develops the continuum governing equations and enables nonreciprocal transition waves with adjustable return positions and the capability of multiple propagations. Section 5 employs singular perturbation analysis to derive traveling wave solutions in both directions, coupled with machine learning. Section 6 investigate nonreciprocal energy transmission and the influence of key parameters. The final section concludes the study and discusses potential applications in active metamaterials. 2 Active bistable system and slow-recovery dynamics We consider a one-dimensional periodic lattice of N identical point masses m with lattice spacing a. Each mass i undergoes longitudinal motion ui (t) and is coupled to its nearest neighbors by linear springs with stiffness k. In addition, each mass is attached to two rigid walls through a pair of bistable elastic elements arranged in parallel (see Fig. 1(d)). The on-site element comprises a passive component and an active component with instantaneous potential energies φ p and φ a , respectively. This construction is motivated by the fact that many biophysical systems alter their energy landscapes under the interference of injected energy. For example, muscle realizes contraction and relaxation through the biologically powered automatic regulation of the binding degree between myosin heads and actin (see Fig. 1(a) and (b)). At its core, this mechanism represents a balance between the active force determined by biological energy and the passive force of the structure at different stages. Such a balance can give rise to bistable energy landscapes in many biological structures, including sarcomeres (see Fig. 1(c)). The variable affected by the injected energy is denoted by si (t). We define si (t) as the active stiffness, in order to represent the active component of the system stiffness coupled to the mechanical displacement ui (t). Altogether, we arrive at a two-variable coupled model for ui (t) and si (t). The physical basis and implications of si (t) are discussed in Appendix A. The total on-site potential is denoted U i ( u i ; s i ) . To obtain a bistable nonlinear potential with tunable asymmetry, we model the restoring force as the derivative of a quartic potential (Khajehtourian and Kochmann, 2021; Veenstra et al., 2024), (1) f i ( u i ; s i ) = ∂ u i U i ( u i ; s i ) = ( u i 2 − L 2 ) ( s i + A 0 ) u i + L δ − 1 ( u i 2 − L 2 ) ( s i − A 1 ) , with constants L > 0 and δ > 0. For brevity, the derivation of Eq. (1) is given in Appendix A. We refer to A 0 > 0 and A 1 > 0 as passive stiffness, which represent the intrinsic structural stiffness of the bistable units, in contrast to active stiffness. The FitzHugh-Nagumo model (FitzHugh, 1955; 1961) is a phenomenological model originally developed for neuronal excitability. Within this framework, we adopt its abstract representation of universal fast-activation and slow-recovery cycles observed in excitable biological systems. This formalism naturally extends to describe the rapid contraction and subsequent slow recovery of sarcomeres in muscle fibers, where rapid mechanical activation is succeeded by a slower metabolic restoration phase. We posit that the active stiffness si (t) evolves on a slow time scale according to (2) s ˙ i = ε ( a 0 − a 1 u i − b s i ) , 0 0 , a 1 > 0 , b > 0 , where the small parameter ε encodes the separation of timescales ensuring recovery is slower than excitation. The coupling term a 1 ui linearly links recovery dynamics to the fast deformation variable ui . The constants a 0, a 1, and b govern respectively the baseline recovery level, sensitivity to fast deformation, and rate of return to equilibrium. Note that the displacement ui (t) is treated as a fast variable, whereas the active stiffness si (t) is defined as a slow recovery variable. This formulation adapts the FitzHugh-Nagumo framework to our bistable mechanical system, where the variation of active stiffness phenomenologically represents the internal restorative state analogous to that of a muscle fiber following contraction. Rewriting Eq. Making the identifications ξ = ε b , B 0 = a 0 / b , and B 1 / L = a 1 / b leads to the slow-recovery law used here, (3) s ˙ i = − ξ ( s i − B 0 + B 1 L − 1 u i ) . where ξ denotes the recovery rate of the active stiffness si (t). The derivation of Eq. (3) can be found in Appendix A. Note that Eqs. (1) and (3) define a phenomenological model that simulates the evolution of sarcomeres during muscle fiber contraction and the associated dynamical characteristics of slow recovery. The assumed dynamical form of the phenomenological equations has already been extensively applied to describing the dynamical processes of active structures (Göktepe et al., 2014). Fig. 3 summarizes the numerical results, including waveforms, wave speeds and wave widths, obtained from the two-variable coupled dimensionless continuum model. It also demonstrates the agreement between the continuum and discrete models, thereby validating the effectiveness of the continuum model. Specifically, we solve the continuum model using the semi-implicit Crank-Nicolson numerical method (Srivastava and Tamsir, 2012) based on finite differences. The relative error field defined by ε r = | f c − f d | / | f d | between the dimensionless continuum model and the discrete model is presented in Fig. 3(a)-(c), where fc and fd represent the numerical results of the continuous and discrete models, respectively. The results confirm the accuracy and validity of the continuum model described by Eqs. (14) and (15). As shown in Fig. 3(a)-(c)i, the error observed in the displacement variation field arises from the intrinsic differences between the discrete and continuum models. Therefore the error is mainly concentrated near the mechanical transition front. In contrast, the error in the active stiffness field shown in Fig. 3(a)-(c)ii primarily originates from the inconsistency in the slow variation of si (t) after the continuum approximation. Both types of errors remain within a reasonable range and exhibit excellent agreement with the theoretical model. Moreover, with the increase of the dimensionless recovery rate ζ from 0.01 to 0.2, we observe a pronounced growth of the relative error field near the end of the lattice. This deviation primarily arises from the mismatch between the slow evolution of si (t) and the initiation time of the backward wave, which results in a slight system desynchronization. A detailed analysis of this phenomenon is provided in Appendix H. Therefore, we focus on the set of parameters ζ, B ¯ 0 and B ¯ 1 that influence the active stiffness. In Fig. 3(d), we present the waveforms and their positions at t ¯ = 150 (forward wave) and t ¯ = 400 (backward wave) as the dimensionless recovery rate ζ increases from 0.1 to 0.315. We find that both the forward and backward waves lag in position when ζ = 0.315 , indicating the slowest propagation speeds. But this lag is substantially more pronounced for the backward wave. This indicates that the dimensionless recovery rate ζ of si (t) influences the forward and backward wave speeds to different extents. The difference constitutes one of the sources of nonreciprocity in the two-variable coupled model. As shown in Fig. 3(e), as B ¯ 0 increases from 0.8 to 1.2, the forward wave speed gradually increases whereas the backward-wave speed gradually decreases, and these distinct trends make the nonreciprocity of the active bistable lattice more pronounced. We also find that increasing B ¯ 1 promotes the wave speeds In Fig. 3(f). Both the forward and backward wave speeds grow as B ¯ 1 increases from 0.8 to 1.2, with the backward wave exhibiting a larger increase. This indicates that the larger the coefficient B ¯ 1 of the first-order term of the dimensionless displacement variable u ¯ in Eq. (15), the stronger the system’s nonreciprocity. Fig. 3(g) shows that as ζ increases from 0.1 to 0.15, the width difference between the forward mechanical wave at t ¯ = 150 and the backward mechanical wave at t ¯ = 400 gradually decreases from 1 to 0. As ζ further increases to 0.315, this width difference gradually rises to 0.5 and then remains stable. In Fig. 3(h), we find that when B ¯ 0 ranges from 0.8 to 1 ( ζ = 0.35 ), the widths of the forward and backward mechanical waves are identical. When B ¯ 0 increases to 1.2, the width difference grows from 0 to 0.8. As B ¯ 1 increases from 0.8 to 0.9 ( ζ = 0.35 ), the width difference between the forward and backward mechanical waves decreases from 1.3125 to 0.3125 in Fig. 3(i). During the increase of B ¯ 1 to 1, the width difference remains constant; as B ¯ 1 continues to increase, the width difference rises again to 1 (corresponding to B ¯ 0 = 1.2 ). Based on the above analysis of the active bistable lattice dynamics, we find that the ranges of ζ, B ¯ 0 , and B ¯ 1 influence the propagation speeds and widths of transition waves. Thus these three parameters constitute key factors in determining the system’s nonreciprocity in the two-variable coupled model. For better characterizing the spatiotemporal dynamics of the bistable lattice, we employ the continuum model and control the initiation time of the backward wave to achieve the optimal outcome in the subsequent analysis (see Movie 3). By using numerical methods, we achieve the forward stopping and backward initiation of transition waves at the intermediate position x ¯ = 50 in the active bistable lattice in Fig. 3(j). The implementation of this case provides a foundation for realizing arbitrary return positions in the lattice. As shown in Fig. 3(k) and (l), building on the controllable return positions, we achieve multiple back-and-forth propagations of transition waves within 25 units over the time intervals from t ¯ = 0 to t ¯ = 700 and 1400, respectively. Taken together, both variables transition from phase A to B and then return by waves that travel at uniform velocities c ¯ f and c ¯ b , establishing robust nonreciprocity quantified by c ¯ f − | c ¯ b | . In this section, unless otherwise specified, the following dimensionless parameter values are used by default: ζ = 0.01 , δ = 2 and A ¯ n = B ¯ n = 1 ( n = 0 , 1 ) . It represents the energetic contribution of passive material components that are independent of activity in active structures such as sarcomeres. The reference passive stiffness, A 1, controls the symmetry of the bistable energy potential well. It corresponds to the asymmetric energy contribution of passive materials in active structures. The bistable displacement scale, L, is defined as the distance between two stable equilibrium points. It measures the actual bistable displacement difference of the coarse-grained sarcomere unit. The shape parameter, δ, controls the degree of asymmetry of the bistable potential energy and the location of the potential barrier.
