These PDEs capture the architecture-dependent coupling introduced by strut connectivity and bending, thereby bridging the discrete nodal equilibrium with a continuum formulation suitable for macroscopic fracture analysis. 3.3 Emergent anomalous kinematics and architecture-dependent couplings A comparison between the derived equilibrium PDEs in the GF framework (Eqs. (10)-(19)) and the classical equilibrium equations for an anisotropic elastic solid reveals fundamental distinctions. For microstructures with square symmetry, the classical homogenization approach will lead to the following equilibrium equations expressed in terms of displacements (21) C 11 ∂ 2 u 1 ∂ x 1 2 + C 66 ∂ 2 u 1 ∂ x 2 2 + ( C 12 + C 66 ) ∂ 2 u 2 ∂ x 1 ∂ x 2 = 0 C 66 ∂ 2 u 2 ∂ x 1 2 + C 11 ∂ 2 u 2 ∂ x 2 2 + ( C 12 + C 66 ) ∂ 2 u 1 ∂ x 1 ∂ x 2 = 0 , in which C 11, C 12, and C 66 are three independent elastic constants. It is noted that the classical equilibrium equation in the x 1 - direction only couples the shear strain ∂ 2 u 2 / ∂ x 1 ∂ x 2 . In contrast, the GF-derived equilibrium equations contain additional coupling terms that are absent in the classical orthotropic description. For example, the equilibrium equations for Design C expressed in Eq. (19) contains ∂ 2 u 2 / ∂ x 1 2 , ∂ 2 u 2 / ∂ x 2 2 and ∂ 2 u 2 / ∂ x 1 ∂ x 2 in the x 1-direction and ∂ 2 u 1 / ∂ x 1 2 , ∂ 2 u 1 / ∂ x 2 2 and ∂ 2 u 1 / ∂ x 1 ∂ x 2 in the x 2-direction. These additional terms are the macroscopic manifestations of the underlying micromechanics — specifically, nodal micro-rotations, strut bending, and non-affine kinematics within the unit cell — features that are smoothed out and lost in traditional homogenization. Unlike generalized continuum theories (e.g., Cosserat or strain-gradient elasticity) that introduce such terms phenomenologically with arbitrary length-scale parameters, the GF framework derives them explicitly from the lattice topology. Consequently, the GF methodology establishes a rigorous constitutive link between the discrete architecture and the macroscopic continuum response without recourse to empirical fitting. 3.4 Derivation of consistent macroscopic boundary conditions A complete continuum description requires boundary conditions (BCs) that are energetically consistent with the governing PDEs. Unlike classical homogenization, where BCs are often applied ad hoc, the GF framework derives them directly from the discrete mechanics of boundary nodes. For Dirichlet (displacement) boundaries, nodal constraints are directly mapped to the continuum field. For Neumann boundaries, we enforce force and moment equilibrium on the primary nodes located at the domain edges. By applying the same continuum limit analysis in Section 3.2 to the equilibrium equations of these boundary nodes, which inherently possess reduced coordination numbers, we obtain macroscopic differential constraints on the displacement gradients (for the derivation details, please refer to Supplementary Materials Section S3). For example, the traction-free condition at the right-hand boundary of three designs (see Fig. S3) yields specific compatibility equations. For Design A, this reduces to the following form: (22) { ∂ u 1 ∂ x 1 = 0 ∂ u 2 ∂ x 1 + ∂ u 1 ∂ x 2 = 0 . For Design B, the traction-free condition at the right-hand boundary takes the form (23) { ∂ u 1 ∂ x 1 + D 1 B ∂ u 2 ∂ x 2 = 0 ∂ u 1 ∂ x 2 + D 2 B ∂ u 2 ∂ x 1 = 0 . For Design C, the traction-free condition at the right-hand boundary takes the form (24) { ∂ u 1 ∂ x 1 + D 11 C ∂ u 2 ∂ x 2 + D 12 C ∂ u 1 ∂ x 2 + D 13 C ∂ u 2 ∂ x 1 = 0 ∂ u 2 ∂ x 1 + D 21 C ∂ u 1 ∂ x 2 + D 22 C ∂ u 1 ∂ x 1 + D 23 C ∂ u 2 ∂ x 2 = 0 . The coefficients D 1 B and D 2 B in Eq. (23) and D 11 C , D 12 C , D 13 C , D 21 C , D 22 C , and D 23 C in Eq. (24) are given in Supplementary Materials Section S3. These boundary conditions ensure consistency between the continuum PDEs and the underlying discrete mechanics, providing well-posedness of the macroscopic boundary-value problem and enabling predictive modeling of fracture processes. 4 Configurational mechanics and crack driving force To establish a rigorous fracture criterion for lattice materials, we derive the crack driving force within the framework of configurational mechanics. The configurational force (or Eshelby force) represents the thermodynamic driving force acting on a defect in the configurational manifold (Özenç et al., 2014). While typically derived for continuous media, we extend this concept to the equivalent continuum description of lattices. The procedure involves: (i) defining the strain energy density of the equivalent continuum, (ii) deriving the work-conjugate stress tensor, and (iii) computing the energy variation with respect to virtual crack extension. For an inhomogeneous continuum, the Helmholtz free-energy density ψ ( F , X ) is a function of not only the deformation gradient F = ( I + u ∇ 0 ) but also the reference coordinates X ( ∇ 0 denotes Hamilton operator in the reference configuration). Then, the gradient of ψ in the reference configuration is: (25) ∇ 0 ψ = ∂ ψ ∂ F : F ∇ 0 + ∂ ψ ∂ X | exp , in which the explicit derivative (with the subscript “exp”) accounts for the inhomogeneity of the continuum. Rearranging and integrating Eq.(25) yields (26) ∫ Ω ( ∇ 0 ψ − P : F ∇ 0 ) d Ω = ∫ Ω ( ∂ ψ ∂ X | exp ) d Ω , where Ω is an arbitrary region in the material, and P = ∂ ψ / ∂ F is the first Piola–Kirchhoff stress tensor. Note P : F ∇ 0 = ( F T P ) · ∇ 0 − F T P · ∇ 0 , the above equation leads to (27) ∫ Ω ( ψ I − F T P ) · ∇ 0 d Ω = ∫ Ω ( − F T P · ∇ 0 + ∂ ψ ∂ X | exp ) d Ω , where I is the identity tensor. In this study, the explicit derivative with respect to X is equivalently rewritten as ( ∂ ψ / ∂ a ) | exp , where vector a is the crack length vector (Gurtin, 2000). The total configurational force acting on a crack is therefore (28) J ^ = ∫ Ω ∂ ψ ∂ a | exp d Ω , where the domain Ω contains the crack. By applying the divergence theorem in Eq. (27) and noting that P · ∇ 0 = 0 , we arrive at (29) J ^ = ∫ Ω ( ψ I − F T P ) · ∇ 0 d Ω = ∫ Γ ( ψ I − F T P ) n d s , in which Γ denotes a contour enclosing the crack tip, and n is the outward unit normal to the contour Γ . The tensor ( ψ I − F T P ) is recognized as the Eshelby stress tensor (Özenç et al., 2014). We verified the path-independence of the integral in Eq. (29) numerically following the scheme in Ref. (Li et al., 1985). For the specific case of a straight crack advancing along unit vector m a = ( 1 , 0 ) , the configurational force reduces to a scalar energy-release rate: (30) J ^ = m a · J ^ = m a · ∮ Γ ( ψ I − F T P ) n d s , which serves as the crack driving force. For elastic materials, the Helmholtz free-energy density equals to the strain energy density, which for the lattice materials is constructed by volume-averaging the discrete strain energies of the constituent struts in a unit cell: (31) U = 1 6 V e ∑ n = 1 N n 1 2 ( u e n ) T k e n u e n + 1 4 V e ∑ m N m 1 2 ( u ′ e m ) T k ′ e m u ′ e m , where V e = 4 l 2 B represents the unit cell volume, k ′ e m and u ′ e m are the stiffness matrix and displacement vector of secondary struts, respectively. For Design A, N n =10 and N m =0; For Design B, N n =10 and N m =16; For design C, N n =10 and N m =8. The weighting factors (1/6 and 1/4) explicitly account for the sharing of primary and secondary struts between adjacent unit cells (as depicted in Fig. 2), ensuring strict energetic consistency between the discrete lattice and the effective continuum. 5 Results and discussion 5.1 Emergent elastic properties from graph-field model The physical consistency of the GF framework is first evidenced by its ability to predict macroscopic effective Poisson’s ratio without phenomenological fitting. As derived in Section 3.4, the traction-free boundary conditions (Eqs. (22)-(24)) spontaneously recover the mathematical form of classical linear elasticity. For example, for a solid under uniaxial tension in the x 1 - direction, the vanishing transverse stress implies ∂ u 1 ∂ x 1 + ν ∂ u 2 ∂ x 2 = 0 , with ν being the effective Poisson’s ratio. By mapping the coefficients of our derived boundary equations (Eqs. (22)-(24)) to this classical form, we extract the effective Poisson’s ratio analytically. For Design A, the decoupled boundary condition implies an effective Poisson’s ratio ν = 0 ​. For Designs B and C, the derived coefficients D 1 B and D 11 C (see Supplementary Materials Section S3) correspond directly to the effective Poisson’s ratios. We verified these GF analytical predictions against the direct finite element (FE) simulations of three lattice strips under uniaxial tension (Fig. 3 ). The excellent agreement across a wide range of geometric parameters demonstrates that the GF framework correctly captures emergent macroscopic properties solely from the unit-cell topology and nodal equilibrium. To further validate the framework’s capability in capturing the macroscopic anisotropy inherent in lattice structures, we utilized the GF model to determine the effective elastic constants under varying loading orientations. Fig. 4 (a) illustrates the load conditions for the uniaxial tensile simulations. Specifically, to evaluate the orientation-dependent stiffness, the lattice unit cell was incrementally rotated counterclockwise from 0° to 360° (Fig. 4(b)), and the effective Young’s modulus was calculated analytically. As demonstrated in Fig. 4(c), the Young’s modulus predictions from the GF method are in excellent agreement with direct high-fidelity FE simulations across all orientations. All three lattice designs exhibit distinct orthotropic symmetry. Notably, these results confirm that the GF framework inherently preserves the directional mechanical properties governed by the underlying lattice symmetry. By establishing this anisotropic elastic baseline, we provide a robust foundation for the subsequent fracture analysis, demonstrating that the framework can accurately resolve direction-dependent stress fields without the need for computationally expensive discrete FE iterations. 5.2 Architecture-dependent crack-tip kinematics We further validate the Graph-Field methodology by comparing its predictions for crack-tip displacement fields against high-fidelity FE simulations performed in Abaqus/Standard. The (direct) 2D FE models, consisting of 151×151 unit cells, were constructed to represent a complete mode-I crack configuration, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The dimension of the entire domain was 151×151 mm2, and the pre-existing crack length a was set to 38mm. To account for the non-negligible shear deformations associated with the finite slenderness of the stouter struts, the individual members in these direct FE models were discretized using two-node linear Timoshenko beam elements. An average mesh size of 0.1mm was utilized, and the shear correction factor for the rectangular cross-sections was uniformly set to 0.85 (Gere and Timoshenko, 1972). In addition to the direct FE models, we also carried out simulations on homogenized FE models made of orthotropic media with elastic constants derived from classical homogenization approach. The displacement contours and the crack opening profiles predicted by the direct FE models and GF models in Fig. 5(a) and (b) show excellent agreement, confirming the accuracy of the GF methodology. In contrast, the homogenized FE model yields displacement fields that deviate notably from the discrete realities (Direct FE and GF) for all designs. This deviation between homogenized FE, Direct FE, and the GF method is particularly pronounced in the crack opening profiles shown in Fig. 5(c), which arises from the architecture-induced couplings missing in the standard homogenized equations (Eqs. (10)-(19)). This discrepancy reveals that lattice topology fundamentally reshapes the near-tip kinematic fields, creating anomalous displacement distributions that cannot be captured by homogenous anisotropic elastic media (Ayatollahi et al., 2022). The influence of lattice topologies on the crack-tip deformation patterns discovered here is consistent with previous experimental observations (Luan et al., 2022). These results demonstrate that architecture dictates the macroscopic fracture kinematics, a phenomenon intrinsically captured by the GF framework but inaccessible to standard homogenization. 5.3 A parameter-free criterion for crack trajectory prediction The prediction of crack trajectories in lattice materials has historically bifurcated into two approaches, each presenting distinct limitations. The first adapts classical fracture criteria (Zeng and Wei, 2017, Ming-Yuan and Hutchinson, 1989)—such as maximum circumferential stress — by employing homogenized stress-intensity factors and empirically fitted parameters (Lohmuller et al., 2018, Bernstein and Hess, 2003). While computationally inexpensive, this homogenization-based approach inherently smooths out the discrete architecture, necessitating extensive calibration to account for local stress concentrations (Romijn and Fleck, 2007). The second approach relies on discrete truss-network simulations (Beex et al., 2014). Although detailed, these models often become computationally prohibitive for large-scale structures and, when simplified to pin-jointed trusses, neglect crucial strut-bending contributions. Consequently, neither framework adequately captures the non-classical, architecture-dependent displacement fields that drive fracture in these systems. Leveraging the high-fidelity kinematics provided by the GF framework, we propose a physics-based criterion for crack-path prediction that requires no empirical fitting. We postulate that crack propagation follows the direction of maximum principal tensile strain, reflecting the local stretching of the underlying struts which is the direct driver of ligament failure. As illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), we evaluate the Green-Lagrange strain tensor E = ( F T F − I ) / 2 along a circular contour Γ of radius r centered at the crack tip O. The outward normal vector n on the contour Γ denotes the candidate propagation direction. For each n ( θ ), we compute the maximum principal strain ε 1 . The predicted crack kinking angle, θ c , is identified as the angle that maximizes ε 1 ​ within the forward sector ( | θ | ≤ 90°). In this study, the evaluation contour is defined at a radius of r = 6l from the crack tip. This specific radius is chosen to accurately capture the local topological driving forces while averting highly localized numerical fluctuations associated with PDE discretization. A rigorous sensitivity analysis (detailed in Fig. S4) confirms that the predicted kinking trajectories remain highly stable for evaluation radii ranging from 3l to 12l. As shown in Fig. 6(b) and (c), the predicted trajectories align precisely with the experimental crack paths observed in CT tests. This suggests that by directly evaluating the maximum principal strain within this topology-encoded continuum field, the GF model naturally identifies the path of maximum local strut stretching — the physical “weakest link” of the network. Consequently, the GF framework effectively projects discrete structural vulnerability onto a continuous spatial field, yielding parameter-free path predictions that align perfectly with the macroscopic principles of orientation-dependent fracture established by Hutchinson and co-workers (Zeng and Wei, 2017, Ming-Yuan and Hutchinson, 1989). To assess the broader applicability of the proposed criterion, specifically its performance in non-orthogonal architectures, we extended our analysis to a classic triangular lattice. Fig. 7 (a) depicts the displacement‑controlled loading setup for a rectangular specimen with a single‑edge crack made of this lattice. The distribution of the maximum principal strain ε 1 ​ around the crack tip, evaluated over a contour radius r = 6l, is presented in Fig. 7(b) (the governing PDEs and corresponding boundary conditions for the triangular lattice are detailed and verified in Section 4 of the Supplementary Material). The predicted trajectory shows good agreement with the experimentally reported result in Fig. 7(c). This consistency across both orthogonal and non-orthogonal topologies confirms that the principle of maximum stretching, when evaluated using the architecture-resolved GF field, constitutes a robust and parameter-free predictor of fracture evolution in lattice materials. 5.4 Validation of the configurational (crack driving) force To rigorously corroborate the physical significance of the derived configurational force J ^ (Eq. (30)), we compare it against Irwin’s classical definition of the energy release rate (Luan et al., 2022, Xie et al., 2004): (32) G = − 1 B ∂ U ∂ a ≈ U ( a + Δ a ) − U ( a − Δ a ) 2 B Δ a , where B is the specimen thickness, a is the pre-crack length, and 2Δa is an increment in crack length. We conduct direct simulations on two high-fidelity FE models to compute the change in total strain energy ΔU resulting from a differential crack extension 2Δa (Fig. 8 (a)) to compute the energy release rate G in Eq. (32) numerically. Simultaneously, we computed J ^ using the path-independent contour integral derived in our GF framework (Fig. 6(b)). For the same configuration, we also computed J ^ using Eq. (30) along a circular path enclosing the crack tip (Fig. 8(b)). The quantitative comparison of the energy release rates given by GF methodology and Irwin’s formula (Fig. 8(c)) demonstrates outstanding agreement, with deviations below 6% across all three lattice designs. This consistency validates two critical aspects: (1) the proposed configurational force is energetically conjugate to crack extension in the discrete lattice; and (2) the path-independence of the Eshelby tensor holds for the equivalent continuum. Consequently, J ^ serves as a rigorous, single-parameter driving force that accurately reflects the thermodynamics of fracture in architected materials. 5.5 New fracture energy determination standards for lattice materials Standard ASTM protocols for CT tests are predicated on LEFM compliance functions derived for homogenous continua. The protocol is now often used to evaluate the fracture toughness of lattice materials (Wang et al., 2024, Shaikeea et al., 2022). The fracture toughness, KIC , was determined from the peak load P using the following formulation: (33) K I C = P i B W f ( a i W ) , where ai =50 mm is the initial crack length, and f ( a i W ) is the calibration function (34) f ( a i W ) = ( 2 + a i W ) [ 0.886 + 4.64 ( a i W ) − 13.32 ( a i W ) 2 + 14.72 ( a i W ) 3 − 5.6 ( a i W ) 4 ] ( 1 − a i W ) 3 / 2 . The critical strain energy release rate, GIC ​, was subsequently calculated as GIC = KIC 2/E*, where E* denotes the effective Young’s modulus of the lattice along the loading direction. Here, we show that this protocol is not applicable to discrete architected materials. Fig. 9 (a) summarizes the experimental force-displacement curves for three lattice designs. Applying the standard ASTM formula (Eq. (33)) to our experimental data yields notably overestimated GIC ​values for Designs B and C (Fig. 9(b)). To address this critical discrepancy, we propose a rigorous methodology that integrates experimental measurements with the GF theoretical framework. By using the critical displacement recorded at crack initiation as the boundary condition for the GF model, we compute the critical configurational force J ^ C according to Eq. (30). This value represents the true intrinsic fracture energy of the lattice. The stark deviation between the ASTM-based estimates and our physics-based calculation underscores the inadequacy of applying continuum standards directly to metamaterials (Wang et al., 2024, Gu et al., 2019). The varying degrees of discrepancy between the classical ASTM estimates and the GF-based calculations across the three designs (Fig. 9) are fundamentally rooted in their topological hierarchies. Design A and the triangular lattice are characterized by “single-level”, uniform topologies where all intersection points contribute equally as primary mechanical sites. This high degree of structural homogeneity allows them to deform in a more affine manner, functioning similarly to the quasi-homogeneous continua assumed by classical LEFM. Consequently, traditional standards yield reasonable fracture toughness approximations for these single-level architectures. In stark contrast, Designs B and C possess a hierarchical, “dual-level” nodal architecture comprising both primary load-bearing nodes and secondary condensing nodes. Under macroscopic loading, these non-uniform topologies generate prominent non-affine microscopic displacements. Because classical phenomenological homogenization and standard ASTM protocols intrinsically smooth out these non-affine kinematics, they severely overestimate the true fracture toughness. By explicitly decoupling primary and secondary degrees of freedom via static condensation, the GF framework captures these non-affine fields, rendering it an indispensable tool for characterizing complex architected systems. 6 Conclusion In this work, we developed and validated a multiscale Graph-Field (GF) framework that resolves the fracture mechanics of lattice materials by rigorously mapping discrete nodal equilibrium to continuum PDEs. This approach bridges the gap between discrete topology and continuum theory, offering a physically transparent alternative to classical homogenization. The key contributions are summarized as follows: 1. Topology-Consistent Continuum Formulation: The GF method rigorously captures architecture-dependent couplings and emergent macroscopic properties (e.g., Poisson’s ratio) that are obscured in conventional homogenization approaches. We demonstrated that topological hierarchy and internal kinematic constraints dictate the macroscopic fracture kinematics, leading to anomalous near-tip displacement fields that deviate fundamentally from classical elliptical predictions. 2. Parameter-Free Crack Path Prediction: We established a propagation rule based on the direction of maximum principal strain, reflecting the local stretching of underlying struts. This criterion accurately predicts complex, kinked crack trajectories observed in experiments without requiring empirical calibration or fitting parameters. 3. Thermodynamically Consistent Fracture Standard: Utilizing Eshelby’s configurational mechanics, we derived a path-independent crack driving force that serves as the intrinsic fracture energy of the lattice. This metric reveals that standard ASTM fracture testing protocols, which rely on homogenous compliance functions, can overestimate the toughness of highly constrained, dual-level lattices by up to 300%. The proposed GF-based methodology thus provides a necessary and robust standard for the experimental characterization of architected systems. Collectively, these findings provide a first-principles foundation for the rational design of fracture-resistant metamaterials. The GF framework paves the way for future extensions into three-dimensional architectures, dynamic fracture phenomena, and nonlinear inelastic responses. Moreover, the framework can naturally be extended to represent spatially heterogeneous structures, such as functionally graded lattices or hybrid designs comprising discrete patches of different topologies, providing a robust continuum tool for multi-functional systems. Furthermore, the analytical nature of the GF field equations provides a mathematically continuous foundation for inverse design, making it highly amenable to integration with artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms. Such synergy enables the high-throughput discovery and generative design of complex structural metamaterials. Finally, by mapping interatomic potentials onto the GF equations, this unified theory may also be extended to molecular-scale systems, such as 2D covalently bound fullerene networks (Hou et al., 2022, Zhao et al., 2023).
